problems.title: [Bug]: phase-server draft handlers accept unbounded wire fields
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: phase-server draft handlers accept unbounded wire fields


## Description

`CreateDraftWithSettings`, `JoinDraftWithPassword`, and `ReconnectDraft` are handled directly in `phase-server` as `ClientMessage` variants. Unlike lobby game frames, they never pass through `lobby_broker::validate_lobby_message` or the Worker parse boundary.

Attackers can send oversized display names, set codes, draft codes, passwords, and player tokens. Those strings are cloned into draft sessions, lobby listings, and subscriber broadcasts.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to a native `phase-server` WebSocket.
2. Send `ClientHello`, then `CreateDraftWithSettings` with a `display_name` longer than 20 characters (or a `set_code` longer than 32 bytes).
3. Observe the draft is created and registered in the lobby list.

Repeat with `JoinDraftWithPassword` using a 65-byte `draft_code`, or `ReconnectDraft` with a 129-byte `player_token`.

## Expected behavior

Draft create/join/reconnect enforce the same size/shape bounds as lobby wire validation before any session or lobby mutation.

## Actual behavior

Draft handlers accept unbounded client-supplied strings.

## Impact

Memory amplification, oversized lobby broadcast payloads, and junk stored in draft/lobby state (DoS / UI breakage).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/draft_wire_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (draft handler guards)
- `crates/server-core/src/draft_session.rs` (regression tests)

problems.title: Draft pick timer is armed once at StartDraft and never renewed — idle pod softlocks after the first pick window
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: Draft pick timer is armed once at StartDraft and never renewed — later pick windows have no timer, and an idle pod softlocks

## Description

The native `phase-server` draft pick timer (`spawn_pick_timer` in `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs`) is a **one-shot**: it is spawned a single time, only when the `StartDraft` action is handled (`if is_start { spawn_pick_timer(...) }`). The spawned task sleeps for the pick duration, auto-picks the first card for any seat that still has a pending pack, broadcasts, and then **ends**. Nothing ever re-arms it:

- The `DraftAction` handler re-arms the timer only on `StartDraft`, not on subsequent picks.
- The timer task's own auto-pick path (`draft_core::session::apply(...)`) does not re-arm a follow-up timer.
- `spawn_pick_timer` has exactly one call site.

A draft has many pick windows (each pack is picked card-by-card across multiple rounds), but only the **first** window is ever protected by a timer.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Start an N-player draft on a native `phase-server`.
2. All players make their first pick quickly (well under the 75s timer).
3. The pack passes; players begin the next pick window.
4. Observe: the single timer, armed at `StartDraft`, fires once at T+75s — auto-picking whatever is pending at that moment (which may be mid-way through a *later* window) — and is then gone for the rest of the draft.

Idle-pod softlock variant:

1. After the timer fires once (or after the first window), a single seat disconnects or goes AFK without picking.
2. No timer is armed for that window, so the pod waits on that seat **indefinitely**. The draft cannot advance.

## Expected behavior

Every pick window is protected by the pick timer: it is (re)armed after each pick while the draft is in `Drafting` status, so a slow/idle/disconnected seat is auto-picked and the draft keeps progressing. A fully idle pod should auto-complete the draft pick-by-pick rather than hang.

## Actual behavior

Only the first pick window has a timer. Later windows have none. The lone timer can also fire in the middle of a later window, auto-picking prematurely. An idle or disconnected seat after the first window stalls the entire pod.

## Impact

- **Softlock:** one idle/disconnected seat hangs the whole draft after the first window.
- **Unfair auto-picks:** the single fixed-offset timer can auto-pick during a later window.
- Affects every multi-pick draft on the native server.

## Component

- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` — `spawn_pick_timer` and the `ClientMessage::DraftAction` handler.

problems.title: SessionManager retains kicked/replaced players' tokens in token-to-game index after a seat mutation
problems.issue_body:
# SessionManager retains kicked/replaced players' tokens in the token-to-game index after a seat mutation

## Description

`SessionManager` keeps a `token_to_game` index (player token → game code) that backs `game_for_token`, used to route a reconnecting client to their game. When the host performs a seat mutation — **kick a player, replace a seat with AI, or remove a seat** — the seat reducer reports the affected tokens in `SeatDelta::invalidated_tokens`, and `GameSession::apply_seat_delta` clears those seats' per-seat token entries (`player_tokens`, `connected`, `decks`, reservations).

But `apply_seat_delta` is a method on `GameSession`; the `token_to_game` index lives on `SessionManager` and is never touched. So after a kick/replace/remove, the invalidated tokens remain mapped to the game in `token_to_game`.

In `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs`, the `SeatMutate` handler computes `kicked_players` from `delta.invalidated_tokens`, calls `session.apply_seat_delta(...)`, and then disconnects those players — without removing their tokens from the manager's index.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Create a multi-seat game; players join (each token is registered in `token_to_game`).
2. The host kicks one player (or replaces their seat with AI).
3. Call `SessionManager::game_for_token` with the kicked player's token.

## Expected behavior

The kicked/replaced token no longer resolves to the game (it was removed from the index along with the seat's other per-seat state).

## Actual behavior

`game_for_token(kicked_token)` still returns the game code. The stale mapping:
- lets a kicked client's token still resolve to a game it is no longer part of (the reconnect then fails downstream with a confusing error, rather than cleanly "not in game"); and
- is never reclaimed, so the index accumulates dead entries over a long-running server's lifetime (one per kicked/replaced seat).

## Impact

Stale index entries and confusing reconnect behavior after host seat edits; unbounded slow growth of `token_to_game` on long-lived servers.

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/session.rs` — `SessionManager` (`token_to_game`, `apply_seat_delta`)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` — the `SeatMutate` handler

problems.title: [Bug]: "Lure" effects ("All creatures able to block ~ do so") only force ONE blocker instead of every able creature — CR
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

The "lure" class — **"All creatures able to block [target/~] do so"** — is parsed as if it were **"must be blocked if able"** and lowered to `StaticMode::MustBeBlocked`. But those two requirements are not equivalent:

- **"must be blocked if able"** (CR 509.1c) is satisfied as soon as the defending player assigns **at least one** legal blocker to the attacker.
- **"all creatures able to block it do so"** (Lure, CR 509.1c) places a *block requirement on every creature able to block it* — **each** such creature must be declared as a blocker of that attacker.

Because the engine collapses the lure requirement into `MustBeBlocked`, a defender who controls several untapped creatures that could all block the lured attacker may legally block with **just one** and hold the rest back. That is illegal under CR 509.1c: every able creature has a "must block this creature" requirement, and a block declaration that obeys fewer requirements than the maximum possible is illegal.

The source card data even models this distinctly — Prized Unicorn carries a `"_PermanentRule":"AllCreaturesMustBlockIt"` rule, separate from the generic must-be-blocked rule — so the "all" semantics is lost only at the engine's parse/lower step. **22 cards** in the dataset carry this rule.

## Cards (a class, not a one-off)

| Card | Oracle text (relevant clause) | Form |
|------|-------------------------------|------|
| **Prized Unicorn** | "All creatures able to block Prized Unicorn do so." | static (self) |
| **Lure** | "Enchant creature. All creatures able to block enchanted creature do so." | static (Aura) |
| **Taunting Elf** | "All creatures able to block Taunting Elf do so." | static (self) |
| **Breaker of Armies** | "All creatures able to block Breaker of Armies do so." | static (self) |
| **Nemesis Mask** | "…All creatures able to block equipped creature do so." | static (Equipment) |
| **Alluring Scent** | "All creatures able to block target creature this turn do so." | one-shot (sorcery) |
| **Curse of the Nightly Hunt** | "All creatures able to block enchanted player's creatures do so." | static (Curse) |

…and ~15 more (22 total carry `AllCreaturesMustBlockIt`).

## Expected

When a lured creature attacks, **every** untapped creature the defending player controls that *can* legally block it (and isn't required elsewhere by a higher-priority restriction) **must** be declared as a blocker of that attacker. A declaration that leaves an able blocker back is rejected, exactly as menace/`MustBlock` requirements are enforced today.

## Actual

The lure clause lowers to `StaticMode::MustBeBlocked`. Combat enforcement treats the requirement as satisfied the moment a single blocker is assigned, so the defender can block with one creature and keep the rest unblocking. The "all" half of the effect is silently dropped.

## Root cause

### 1. Parser collapses lure → MustBeBlocked (the wrong equivalence)

`crates/engine/src/parser/oracle_effect/mod.rs:4462-4514` — `try_parse…

problems.title: Canopy Gargantuan: upkeep trigger fires but applies no +1/+1 counters
problems.issue_body:
## Card

**Canopy Gargantuan** — {5}{G}{G}, 7/7 Dragon, Flying, ward {2}

> At the beginning of your upkeep, put a number of +1/+1 counters on each other creature you control equal to that creature's toughness.

## Bug

The triggered ability **does** trigger at the beginning of the controller's upkeep, but it does **not** place any +1/+1 counters on the other creatures the controller has on the battlefield. The trigger resolves as a no-op.

## Root cause

This is a "for each matching creature, place counters equal to **that creature's** toughness" effect — the count must be evaluated **per creature**. There are two distinct problems:

### 1. The resolver evaluates the count once and applies it uniformly

`crates/engine/src/game/effects/counters.rs` — `resolve_add_all` (CR 122.1):

```rust
Effect::PutCounterAll { counter_type, count, target } => {
    let resolved =
        crate::game::quantity::resolve_quantity_with_targets(state, count, ability).max(0) as u32;
    (counter_type.clone(), resolved, target.clone())
}
```

The count is resolved a **single time** into `counter_num`, then that one number is applied to every matching object in the loop:

```rust
for obj_id in matching_ids {
    add_counter_with_replacement(state, ability.controller, obj_id, counter_type.clone(), counter_num, events);
}
```

There is no per-object evaluation path. Even with a correct quantity reference, every creature would receive the same number of counters rather than a count derived from its own toughness.

### 2. The parser maps "that creature's toughness" to the wrong scope

`data/card-data.json` parses the count as:

```json
"count": {
  "type": "Ref",
  "qty": { "type": "Toughness", "scope": { "type": "CostPaidObject" } }
}
```

`CostPaidObject` is incorrect here — there is no cost-paid object on this trigger, so the quantity resolves to 0, and every creature receives 0 counters (matching the observed "no counters applied" symptom). The reference should point at the **per-iteration object** being counted by `PutCounterAll`, not a cost-paid object.

## Expected behavior

On the controller's upkeep, each *other* creature they control gains a number of +1/+1 counters equal to that creature's own (current) toughness.

## Fix direction

This is a building-block gap, not a one-off. `PutCounterAll` (and likely sibling `*All` effects) needs a way to express a **per-object count** that re-evaluates the quantity against each matching object in the loop. That requires:

- A typed scope/reference meaning "the object currently receiving counters in this `*All` iteration" (the per-iteration self-reference), and
- `resolve_add_all` evaluating the quantity inside the loop per `obj_id` when the count references that per-iteration object (rather than resolving once up front).

The parser then needs to map "that creature's toughness" in this construction to that per-iteration reference instead of `CostPaidObject`.

## Files

- `crates/engine/src/game/effects/counters.rs` (`resolv…

problems.title: [Bug]: "Provoke" / "blocks ~ this turn if able" forces the creature to block ANY attacker, not the specific one — CR 702
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

The "forced block" class — Provoke (CR 702.39) and one-shot/triggered **"target creature blocks ~ this turn if able"** effects — only requires the chosen creature to block **some** attacker, not the **specific** attacker named by the effect. A defending player can satisfy the requirement by blocking a *different* (weaker) attacker, completely dodging the provoke.

The effect's "block **it / ~ / this creature**" antecedent — i.e. *which* attacker must be blocked — is dropped at parse time, and the resolver grants a **generic** `StaticMode::MustBlock` ("this creature must block *any* attacker if able"). Combat validation then accepts any block assignment that has the creature blocking anything at all.

## Cards (a class, not a one-off)

- **Provoke** keyword (CR 702.39): "Whenever this creature attacks, you may have target creature defending player controls **block it** this combat if able. If you do, untap it." — Krosan Vorine, Bull Hippo, Charging Rhino, Nemesis (and ~12 Provoke cards present in the dataset).
- One-shot / triggered forced block: **Master Warcraft**, **Nemesis Mask**, and other "**target creature blocks [this creature/it] this turn if able**" effects.
- The source card data models this distinctly as the `MustBlockAttacker` permanent rule — **35 cards** carry it, separate from the generic `MustBlock` rule (29 cards).

## Expected

When card X forces creature C to "block X this turn if able," C carries a requirement to block **X specifically**. Per CR 509.1c, if C *can* legally block X, the blocker declaration is illegal unless C is declared as a blocker **of X**. Blocking a different attacker — or not blocking — does not satisfy the requirement. (If C cannot legally block X, e.g. X has flying and C lacks reach, the requirement imposes nothing.)

## Actual

`Effect::ForceBlock { target }` captures only the creature being forced; the attacker it must block is discarded. `force_block.rs` grants a generic `MustBlock`, and combat enforcement is satisfied as long as C blocks *any* attacker — so:

- C can block a small irrelevant attacker instead of the provoker (dodge).
- Conversely, generic `MustBlock` can wrongly force C to block *some other* attacker even when the provoke requirement should not apply.

## Root cause

### 1. The "block it / ~" antecedent is dropped at parse time

`crates/engine/src/parser/oracle_effect/imperative.rs:5522-5533` recognizes "blocks ~/it/this creature this turn/combat if able" and emits `ImperativeFamilyAst::ForceBlock`, then lowers (`imperative.rs:6341`) to `Effect::ForceBlock { target }` — where `target` is the *forced creature*. The object it must block ("~"/"it"/"this creature") is never captured.

`crates/engine/src/types/ability.rs:6199` — `ForceBlock { target: TargetFilter }` has no field for the attacker that must be blocked.

### 2. The resolver grants a generic, attacker-agnostic MustBlock

`crates/engine/src/game/effects/force_block.rs:30-40`:

```rust
// CR 509.1c: Grant MustBlock u…

problems.title: [Bug]: Native LookupJoinTarget bypasses lobby wire validation
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: Native LookupJoinTarget bypasses lobby wire validation


## Description

The Cloudflare Worker validates `LookupJoinTarget` via `parse_lobby_client_message` → `validate_lobby_message`. Native `phase-server` handles `LookupJoinTarget` directly for both Full and LobbyOnly modes without running the same bounds first.

Attackers can send oversized game codes, passwords, display names, and reservation tokens. Those strings are cloned through lobby lookup, seat reservation, and subscriber fan-out paths.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to a native `phase-server` WebSocket (Full or LobbyOnly).
2. Send `ClientHello`, then `LookupJoinTarget` with a 65-byte `game_code` (or 129-byte `release_reservation_token`).
3. Observe the server processes the frame instead of rejecting it at the boundary.

The Worker path rejects the same payload at parse time.

## Expected behavior

Native lookup/join-target handling enforces the same wire bounds as the broker before any lobby mutation.

## Actual behavior

The native handler skips lobby wire validation on entry.

## Impact

Memory amplification on reservation and lookup paths; oversized strings stored in lobby state (DoS).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/lookup_join_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (LookupJoinTarget guard)
- `crates/server-core/src/reconnect.rs` (regression tests)

problems.title: [Bug]: Legacy CreateGame/JoinGame accept unbounded deck payloads
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: Legacy CreateGame/JoinGame accept unbounded deck payloads


## Description

Full-mode settings-based create/join enforce deck bounds via `lobby_broker::guard_inbound`. The legacy `CreateGame` and `JoinGame` API sends only a `DeckData` payload and goes straight to `resolve_deck` without bounding card lists or card-name lengths first.

Attackers can send decks with thousands of entries or multi-kilobyte card names, forcing expensive clone and card-database resolution work before rejection.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to a native `phase-server` in Full mode.
2. Send `ClientHello`, then `CreateGame` with `main_deck` containing 501+ card name strings.
3. Observe the server begins deck resolution instead of rejecting at the wire boundary.

## Expected behavior

Legacy create/join reject oversized deck payloads before `resolve_deck`.

## Actual behavior

Unbounded deck lists reach card-database resolution.

## Impact

CPU and memory amplification on the legacy API path (DoS).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/legacy_deck_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (CreateGame/JoinGame guards)
- `crates/server-core/src/deck_resolve.rs` (regression tests)

problems.title: [Bug]: Emote broadcasts accept unbounded client strings
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: Emote broadcasts accept unbounded client strings


## Description

`ClientMessage::Emote` is handled directly in `phase-server`. The emote string is cloned into a `ServerMessage::Emote` and fan-out to every connected player in the game without length or control-character bounds.

Attackers in an active game can send multi-kilobyte emotes that are cloned once per peer, amplifying memory use across all participants on every send.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Join a native `phase-server` game as two players.
2. Send `Emote` with a 10,000-character string.
3. Observe the server clones and broadcasts the payload to the other player.

## Expected behavior

Emotes are bounded (character count, no control chars) before broadcast.

## Actual behavior

Unbounded emote strings are cloned and fan-out to all game connections.

## Impact

Per-player memory amplification on every emote send (DoS / UI breakage).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/emote_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (Emote handler guard)
- `crates/server-core/src/protocol.rs` (regression tests)

problems.title: Goad redirect (CR 701.15b) counts unattackable players (phased-out / teammates / protection-from-everything) as valid 'o
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

In `crates/engine/src/game/combat.rs`, `declare_attackers` enforces the goad redirect — CR 701.15b: *a goaded creature must attack a player other than the goading player **if able*** — by checking whether any **non-eliminated** non-goading player exists:

```rust
let non_eliminated_opponents: Vec<PlayerId> = state
    .players
    .iter()
    .filter(|p| p.id != state.active_player && !state.eliminated_players.contains(&p.id))
    .map(|p| p.id)
    .collect();
// ...
let has_non_goading_target = non_eliminated_opponents
    .iter()
    .any(|pid| !goading_players.contains(pid));
if has_non_goading_target {
    return Err(/* goaded ... must attack a different player if able */);
}
```

`non_eliminated_opponents` only excludes the active player and eliminated players. It still includes **teammates, phased-out players, and players with protection from everything** — none of which are legal attack targets. So a goaded creature is wrongly judged "able" to attack one of those players and is illegally forced off a goading player toward a target it cannot actually attack.

## What the CR requires

- CR 701.15b: "A creature that's goaded attacks a player other than the controller of the goad effect during its controller's combat phase if able." The **"if able"** clause means the redirect only applies when the creature can legally attack such a player.
- CR 506.2 / 506.3: legal attack targets are opposing players (and their planeswalkers/battles) that are not phased out, not protected, etc. The engine's `get_valid_attack_targets` already computes exactly this set (excluding the active player, eliminated players, teammates, phased-out players, and protection-from-everything players).

## Steps to reproduce

1. 3-player game. P0 (active) controls a creature goaded by P1.
2. The only other opponent, P2, is phased out (or is a teammate, or has protection from everything) — i.e. not a legal attack target.
3. Declare the goaded creature attacking P1.

## Expected behavior

The declaration is legal: there is no **attackable** non-goading player, so attacking the goading player P1 is allowed (CR 701.15b "if able").

## Actual behavior

The declaration is rejected with `"... is goaded by ... and must attack a different player if able (CR 701.15b)"`, because the unattackable P2 is counted as a valid "different player."

## Suggested fix

Measure "able" against `get_valid_attack_targets(state)` (the canonical valid-attack-target set) rather than every non-eliminated player.

problems.title: [Bug]: Game session Reconnect accepts unbounded wire fields
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: Game session Reconnect accepts unbounded wire fields


## Description

`ReconnectDraft` is bounded by `draft_wire_guard::guard_reconnect_draft`, but Full-mode game `Reconnect` is handled directly in `phase-server` without validating `game_code` or `player_token` lengths first.

Attackers can send multi-kilobyte codes and tokens that are cloned into session lookup, reconnect-manager paths, and connection registration before rejection.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to a native `phase-server` in Full mode.
2. Send `ClientHello`, then `Reconnect` with a 65-byte `game_code` or 129-byte `player_token`.
3. Observe the server processes the frame instead of rejecting at the boundary.

## Expected behavior

Game reconnect enforces the same token/code bounds as lobby join and draft reconnect before session work.

## Actual behavior

Unbounded `game_code` and `player_token` reach session handlers.

## Impact

Memory amplification on reconnect paths (DoS).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/game_reconnect_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (Reconnect guard)
- `crates/server-core/src/reconnect.rs` (regression tests)

problems.title: [Bug]: "This creature can't be blocked by more than one creature" (Stalking Tiger class) is unparsed and unenforced — an
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

The per-creature block **maximum** — **"[this creature] can't be blocked by more than N creature(s)"** (Stalking Tiger, Outland Colossus, Tahngarth, …) — has no representation in the engine. The clause never parses into a static, and combat declaration imposes no upper bound, so the affected creature can be blocked by **any number** of creatures.

This is distinct from the two block-count mechanics the engine *does* model:

- `StaticMode::MaxBlockersEachCombat { max }` — a **global** cap ("no more than N creatures can block **each combat**", Silent Arbiter / Dueling Grounds). Applies to the whole combat, not one attacker.
- `StaticMode::CantBeBlockedExceptBy { kind: MinBlockers { min } }` — menace, a **minimum** ("can't be blocked except by N **or more**").

Neither expresses "this specific attacker may be blocked by **at most** N creatures." That maximum is the inverse of menace and is currently dropped.

## Cards (a class, not a one-off)

The source card data carries a dedicated `CantBeBlockedByMoreThanOne` permanent rule on **31 cards**, including:

- **Stalking Tiger** — "Stalking Tiger can't be blocked by more than one creature."
- **Outland Colossus** — "…can't be blocked by more than one creature."
- **Tahngarth, First Mate** — "…can't be blocked by more than one creature."
- **Sunder Shaman**, **Professional Wrestler**, **Safewright Cavalry**, **Vorrac Battlehorns**, **Norwood Riders**, **Stalking Tiger**, etc.

(The canonical text is "more than one"; parameterizing the maximum covers any "more than N" printings.)

## Expected

A creature with "can't be blocked by more than N creatures" may be declared as blocked by **at most N** creatures (CR 509.1b). A blocker declaration assigning more than N blockers to it is illegal.

## Actual

The clause is not recognized by the static parser, so no static ability is produced. Combat's `validate_blockers_for_player` enforces a global per-combat cap (`MaxBlockersEachCombat`) and menace **minimums**, but never a per-attacker **maximum**. The restriction is silently a no-op — the creature can be ganged up on by any number of blockers.

## Root cause

### 1. No `StaticMode` for a per-creature blocker maximum

`crates/engine/src/types/statics.rs` — the combat-count statics are `MaxAttackersEachCombat { max }` / `MaxBlockersEachCombat { max }` (global, ~line 442-450) and `CantBeBlockedExceptBy { kind: BlockExceptionKind::MinBlockers { min } }` (menace minimum). There is no "at most N may block **this** creature" variant.

### 2. The parser has no branch for the phrase

`crates/engine/src/parser/oracle_static/evasion.rs` handles "can't be blocked except by …" via `classify_block_exception` (producing `MinBlockers`/`Quality`), but nothing matches "can't be blocked by more than N creature(s)". A grep of `crates/engine/src/parser` for "blocked by more than" / "more than one creature" returns no production parser path — the clause falls through.

### 3. Combat declaration enforces no per-attacker …

problems.title: Mass Effect::PhaseIn ignores its filter and phases in every phased-out permanent (CR 702.26b / 109.4)
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

`resolve_phase_in` (`crates/engine/src/game/effects/phase_out.rs`) resolves a mass (non-targeted) `Effect::PhaseIn` through `collect_phase_in_targets`, which **ignores the effect's filter entirely** and phases in *every* phased-out permanent on the battlefield:

```rust
// For mass phase-in effects ... Rely on the caller-supplied filter to
// narrow (e.g., "all phased-out permanents you control"). ... For
// now, a mass phase-in with a controller-scoped filter is approximated
// by scanning all battlefield objects and checking controller.
let _ = (ability, target);   // <- filter discarded
state
    .battlefield
    .iter()
    .copied()
    .filter(|id| state.objects.get(id).is_some_and(|obj| obj.is_phased_out()))
    .collect()
```

The comment says it should narrow by the caller's filter and even claims it is "approximated by ... checking controller" — but the code does neither. Any mass phase-in resolves against **all** phased-out permanents regardless of the card's scope.

## What the CR requires

- CR 702.26b: a phased-out permanent is treated as though it doesn't exist, *except* for "rules and effects that specifically mention phased-out permanents" — i.e. phasing-in effects must be able to see and select phased-out permanents.
- CR 109.4 / 700: an effect's controller/type scope ("each phased-out permanent **you control**") restricts which permanents it affects.

So a card that phases in *your* phased-out permanents must not also phase in an opponent's.

## Failure mode

A "phase in each phased-out permanent you control"-style effect, resolved while an opponent also has phased-out permanents, phases in **both** players' permanents (the opponent's permanents return early, outside their controller's untap step).

## Suggested fix

Apply the effect's filter to the phased-out permanents. The filter choke point (`matches_target_filter`) hides phased-out objects, so add a sibling that evaluates the filter while bypassing only the phased-out exclusion (the same `filter_inner_for_object` path `matches_target_filter_in_owner_zone` already uses), and use it in `collect_phase_in_targets` so controller/type scope is honored.

## Component

- `crates/engine/src/game/effects/phase_out.rs` — `collect_phase_in_targets`
- `crates/engine/src/game/filter.rs` — phased-out-aware filter evaluation

problems.title: [Bug]: Spectator routes accept unbounded game/draft codes
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: Spectator routes accept unbounded game/draft codes


## Description

`SpectatorJoin` and `SpectateDraft` are handled directly in native `phase-server` without validating code length at the wire boundary.

An attacker can send oversized `game_code` / `draft_code` values that are cloned into lookup and identity paths before rejection, causing avoidable memory amplification.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to native `phase-server`.
2. Send `ClientHello`, then `SpectatorJoin` or `SpectateDraft` with a 65-byte code.
3. Observe the server processes the request path instead of rejecting immediately.

## Expected behavior

Spectator routes enforce the same token/code bounds as other lobby and reconnect paths before lookup work.

## Actual behavior

Unbounded spectator codes reach session/draft lookup handlers.

## Impact

Memory and CPU amplification on spectator endpoints (DoS vector).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/spectator_wire_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (SpectatorJoin/SpectateDraft guards)
- `crates/server-core/src/reconnect.rs` (regression tests)

problems.title: [Bug]: DraftAction accepts unbounded draft_code
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: DraftAction accepts unbounded draft_code


## Description

`CreateDraftWithSettings`, `JoinDraftWithPassword`, and `ReconnectDraft` validate wire fields via `draft_wire_guard`. `DraftAction` is handled on a separate path and previously accepted unbounded `draft_code` values before draft session lookup and action dispatch.

Attackers can send multi-kilobyte draft codes that are cloned through `handle_draft_action`, pack generation, and lobby update paths.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to native `phase-server` in Full mode and join a draft.
2. Send `DraftAction` with a `draft_code` longer than 64 bytes.
3. Observe the server processes the frame instead of rejecting at the boundary.

## Expected behavior

`DraftAction` enforces the same draft code bounds as other draft wire paths before any session mutation.

## Actual behavior

Unbounded `draft_code` reaches draft action handlers.

## Impact

Memory and CPU amplification on active draft sessions (DoS).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/draft_action_wire_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (DraftAction guard)
- `crates/server-core/src/draft_session.rs` (regression tests)

problems.title: [Bug]: CreateGameWithSettings ai_seats bypass wire validation
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: CreateGameWithSettings ai_seats bypass wire validation


## Description

Full-mode `CreateGameWithSettings` validates the host lobby fields through `lobby_broker::guard_inbound`, but `ai_seats` are stripped when projecting to `LobbyClientMessage` and never bounded.

Attackers can send huge `ai_seats` arrays, oversized starter-deck names, and unbounded nested deck lists. The server clones those payloads while setting up AI opponents.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to native `phase-server` in Full mode.
2. Send `ClientHello`, then `CreateGameWithSettings` with `ai_seats` containing 6+ entries or a nested deck list with 501 main-deck cards.
3. Observe the server begins AI deck setup instead of rejecting at the wire boundary.

## Expected behavior

`ai_seats` are bounded before any AI deck resolve or session mutation.

## Actual behavior

`ai_seats` bypass lobby wire validation entirely.

## Impact

Memory and CPU amplification during game creation (DoS).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/ai_seats_wire_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (Full-mode create guard)
- `crates/server-core/src/protocol.rs` (regression tests)

problems.title: Quandrix, the Proof: cascade does not trigger (its own cast, and granted to instants/sorceries)
problems.issue_body:
## Card

**Quandrix, the Proof** — {4}{G}{U}, Legendary Creature — Elder Dragon, 6/6, Flying, trample

> Cascade *(When you cast this spell, exile cards from the top of your library until you exile a nonland card that costs less. You may cast it without paying its mana cost. Put the exiled cards on the bottom in a random order.)*
> Instant and sorcery spells you cast from your hand have cascade.

*(Reported as "Qundrix, the Proof" — this is the correct card.)*

## Bug

Two separate cascade failures, both observed in-game:

1. **Its own cascade does not trigger when cast.** Casting Quandrix, the Proof does not fire its printed Cascade ability — no cards are exiled, no free cast offered.
2. **Granted cascade does not trigger.** While Quandrix is on the battlefield, casting instant or sorcery spells from hand does not give them cascade — no cascade trigger fires for those spells.

## Parse (engine-authoritative)

The card parses correctly into both the printed keyword and the grant static (`data/card-data.json`):

```json
"keywords": ["Cascade", "Flying", "Trample"],
"static_abilities": [{
  "mode": { "CastWithKeyword": { "keyword": "Cascade" } },
  "affected": {
    "type": "Or",
    "filters": [
      { "type": "Typed", "type_filters": ["Instant"], "controller": "You",
        "properties": [{ "type": "InZone", "zone": "Hand" }] },
      { "type": "Typed", "type_filters": ["Sorcery"], "controller": "You",
        "properties": [{ "type": "InZone", "zone": "Hand" }] }
    ]
  },
  "active_zones": ["Battlefield"]
}]
```

So this is **not** a parser-coverage gap — the AST is correct for both mechanics. The failure is at resolution time.

## What I traced

The generic machinery to support both paths exists:

- `effective_spell_keywords` (`crates/engine/src/game/casting.rs:870`) merges the spell's printed `obj.keywords` with `granted_spell_keywords` (the `CastWithKeyword` statics), so a granted Cascade should appear alongside a printed one.
- The cascade trigger synthesis (`crates/engine/src/game/triggers.rs:1454-1492`, CR 702.85a/c) counts `Keyword::Cascade` from `effective_spell_keywords` on the `SpellCast` event and enqueues one `Effect::Cascade` per instance.
- There is a passing unit test `statically_granted_cascade_triggers_for_cast_spell` (`triggers.rs:11787`) asserting that a `CastWithKeyword { Cascade }` static enqueues a Cascade trigger.

## Hypothesis for bug 2 (granted cascade)

The passing test grants cascade with an `affected` filter that has **no zone restriction** (just `Sorcery` + `controller: You`). Quandrix's parsed filter additionally carries an `InZone { zone: Hand }` property.

`granted_spell_keywords` (`casting.rs:703-738`) computes `origin_zone = pending_cast_origin_zone_for(...).unwrap_or(spell_obj.zone)` and passes it to `spell_object_matches_filter_from_state`. If the `InZone { zone: Hand }` property is evaluated against the spell's **current** zone (Stack, once it's been put on the stack to be cast) rather than against the c…

problems.title: [Bug]: SeatMutate accepts unbounded AI deck payloads
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: SeatMutate accepts unbounded AI deck payloads


## Description

`SeatMutate` supports nested `DeckChoice` payloads for AI seats (`Random`, `Named`, and `DeckList`). This path is handled directly in `phase-server` and previously had no wire-level bounds for seat indices, named deck strings, or nested deck lists.

An attacker can send oversized `SeatMutate::SetKind(Ai { deck: DeckList(...) })` payloads that reach reducer/deck-resolution paths and clone-heavy logic before rejection.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to native `phase-server` in Full mode as host.
2. Send `SeatMutate` with `SetKind` AI `DeckChoice::Named` using a 129-byte name, or `DeckChoice::DeckList` containing 501+ main-deck entries.
3. Observe the server processes reducer path instead of rejecting at wire boundary.

## Expected behavior

`SeatMutate` validates seat index and AI deck payload bounds before reducer apply/deck resolution.

## Actual behavior

Unbounded mutation payloads are accepted into server-side mutation handlers.

## Impact

Memory and CPU amplification on host seat-edit endpoints (DoS).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/seat_mutation_wire_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (`SeatMutate` guard)
- `crates/server-core/src/protocol.rs` (regression tests)

problems.title: Ranked Ladder + Match History (competitive progression)
problems.issue_body:
## Problem
Multiplayer matches currently feel session-based without persistent progression. Players lack a clear way to track skill growth, compare performance, or review recent results. This limits retention and competitive engagement.

## Proposal
Add a first-class Ranked experience with:

- Skill rating (MMR/ELO-style) updated after ranked matches
- Matchmaking biased toward similar rating
- Persistent match history per player
- Seasonal ladder and leaderboard
This should build on existing multiplayer/session infrastructure rather than changing core gameplay rules.

## Goals

- Create a meaningful long-term progression loop
- Improve matchmaking quality for competitive games
- Give players visibility into performance trends
- Enable basic competitive identity (rank/tier/profile stats)

## Acceptance Criteria

-  Players can join a ranked queue and be matched by rating
-  Ratings update correctly after ranked games and persist across sessions
-  Players can view current rank/rating and recent match history
-  Leaderboard shows season standings
-  Disconnect/forfeit outcomes are handled consistently
-  Feature is guarded by a config/flag for phased rollout

problems.title: [Bug]: DraftAction accepts unbounded action payloads
problems.issue_body:
## Description

`draft_wire_guard::guard_draft_action` bounds `draft_code` only. The `DraftAction` body can still carry unbounded `SubmitDeck.main_deck` lists, oversized `Pick.card_instance_id` strings, and other large nested fields that are cloned through `handle_draft_action` and draft reducers (for example `validate_limited_deck` over megabyte-scale lists).

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to native `phase-server` in Full mode and join a draft pod.
2. Send `DraftAction` with `SubmitDeck` whose `main_deck` contains 501+ card name strings (or a `Pick` with a 129-byte `card_instance_id`).
3. Observe the server begins draft session work instead of rejecting at the wire boundary.

## Expected behavior

`DraftAction` rejects oversized payloads before draft session lookup and reducer work.

## Actual behavior

Unbounded action bodies reach draft handlers.

## Impact

CPU and memory amplification on active draft sessions (DoS).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/draft_action_payload_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (DraftAction guard)

problems.title: Add live multiplayer spectator mode (privacy-safe)
problems.issue_body:
## Description
Game spectating is not currently available for live multiplayer matches, even though protocol/client scaffolding exists and draft spectating is already implemented. This blocks key use cases like tournament observing, coaching, and community viewing.

We should implement a backend-driven spectator mode for active games that allows read-only observers to receive game updates without exposing hidden information.


## Scope

- Support spectator join for active games by game_code.
- Register/unregister spectator sockets during game lifecycle.
- Send initial filtered game snapshot + ongoing filtered state updates to spectators.
- Enforce strict read-only behavior for spectators (no game actions).
- Ensure disconnect and game-end cleanup works reliably.

## Acceptance criteria

- Spectator can join an in-progress game with a valid game code.
- Spectator receives continuous state updates for that game.
- Spectator cannot perform player actions.
- Hidden zones/info remain private to non-owners (no leakage).
- Spectator disconnects do not affect player sessions.
- Server handles multiple spectators without breaking gameplay.

problems.title: [Bug]: Legacy JoinGame accepts unbounded game_code
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: Legacy JoinGame accepts unbounded game_code

## Description

Full-mode settings-based join enforces `game_code` bounds via `lobby_broker::validate_lobby_message`. Legacy `JoinGame` only runs `guard_legacy_deck` on the deck; an oversized `game_code` is cloned through `resolve_deck` and session lookup before rejection.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to native `phase-server` in Full mode.
2. Send `ClientHello`, then `JoinGame` with a 65-byte `game_code` and a valid small deck.
3. Observe the server begins deck resolution instead of rejecting at the boundary.

## Expected behavior

Legacy join rejects oversized `game_code` before `resolve_deck`.

## Actual behavior

Unbounded `game_code` reaches card-database resolution and session handlers.

## Impact

CPU and memory amplification on the legacy join path (DoS).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/legacy_join_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (JoinGame guard)

problems.title: Parser: 'gain lifelink' misroutes to life-gain (substring "life") and falls to Unimplemented
[Bug]: Native ClientHello accepts unbounded version strings
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: Native ClientHello accepts unbounded version strings

## Description

The lobby broker validates `client_version` and `build_commit` via `validate_lobby_message`, but native `phase-server` handled `ClientHello` in `classify_hello_gate` and cloned unbounded strings into `SocketIdentity` before any size check. Every new WebSocket connection hits this path first.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to native `phase-server` (Full or LobbyOnly).
2. Send `ClientHello` with a 129-byte `client_version` or `build_commit`.
3. Observe the server stores the handshake instead of rejecting at the boundary.

The lobby broker path rejects the same payload at parse time.

## Expected behavior

`ClientHello` enforces the same token bounds as lobby wire validation before cloning identity fields.

## Actual behavior

Unbounded version strings are cloned on accept.

## Impact

Memory amplification on every connection (DoS).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/client_hello_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (handshake gate)

problems.title: feed-scraper: Watery Grave, Blood Crypt, Sacred Foundry infer only one of their two colors
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

`infer_colors` in `crates/feed-scraper/src/scrape.rs` derives a decklist's colors from its lands. For Ravnica shock lands it adds the **primary** color in a first `match`, then the **off-color** in a second `match`:

```rust
match entry.name.as_str() {
    "Hallowed Fountain" | "Temple Garden" | "Godless Shrine" => add("W"),
    "Watery Grave" | "Steam Vents" | "Breeding Pool" => add("U"),
    "Blood Crypt" | "Overgrown Tomb" => add("B"),
    "Sacred Foundry" | "Stomping Ground" => add("R"),
    _ => {}
}
// Second color for dual lands
match entry.name.as_str() {
    "Hallowed Fountain" | "Watery Grave" => add("U"),
    "Steam Vents" | "Sacred Foundry" => add("R"),
    "Blood Crypt" | "Godless Shrine" => add("B"),
    "Breeding Pool" | "Temple Garden" | "Overgrown Tomb" | "Stomping Ground" => add("G"),
    _ => {}
}
```

Three lands are grouped in the second match under a color **equal to their primary**, so `add` (which de-dupes) never records their off-color:

- **Watery Grave** (U/B): first `add("U")`, second `add("U")` → infers only **U** (missing B)
- **Blood Crypt** (B/R): first `add("B")`, second `add("B")` → infers only **B** (missing R)
- **Sacred Foundry** (R/W): first `add("R")`, second `add("R")` → infers only **R** (missing W)

## Steps to reproduce

Scrape (or build) a deck whose only colored source is one of those shock lands. The inferred `colors` contains a single color instead of two.

## Expected behavior

Each shock land infers both of its colors: Watery Grave → U,B; Blood Crypt → B,R; Sacred Foundry → R,W (the other seven are already correct).

## Actual behavior

Watery Grave, Blood Crypt, and Sacred Foundry each infer only their primary color, so decks relying on them for a color are mis-tagged.

## Suggested fix

Regroup the second match by each land's actual off-color (Watery Grave → B, Blood Crypt → R, Sacred Foundry → W).

## Component

- `crates/feed-scraper/src/scrape.rs` — `infer_colors`.

problems.title: [Card Bug] Pawpatch Recruit triggers when targeting Ouroboroid with my own Innkeeper's Talent's level 1 ability
problems.issue_body: ### Card(s) Pawpatch Recruit, Innkeeper's Talent, Ouroboroid ### Build/version v0.1.42 4e7b151 ### Game mode AI ### Actual behavior At the beginning of combat Ouroboroid and Innkeeper's Talent trigger. Upon resolving Innkeeper's Talent first, targeting Ouroboroid, Pawpatch Recruit's ability is falsely triggered. Trying to resolve this Pawpatch Recruit trigger and targeting the Ouroboroid again will trigger Pawpatch Recruit once more, resulting in a loop. ### Expected behavior Since Pawpatch Recruit only triggers on spells and abilities an opponent controls: At the beginning of combat Ouroboroid and Innkeeper's Talent trigger. Upon resolving Innkeeper's Talent first, targeting Ouroboroid. Ouroboroid gets a +1/+1 counter. Next, resolving Ouroboroid's trigger adds X +1/+1 counters to each creature on the battlefield where X is its current power (initial power + a +1/+1 counter) Pawpatch never triggers, thus never leading to the loop ### Steps to reproduce Ouroboroid, Innkeeper's Talent (level 1) on the Battlefield 3 Forests on the Battlefield Pawpatch Recruit in Hand 1. Cast Pawpatch Recruit (either for G or 2G) 2. Go to beginning of combat 3. Initiate the trigger loop ### Logs/screenshots/game-state

problems.title: feat(engine): implement Impending keyword end-to-end (Duskmourn, CR 702.176)
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

Implement the **Impending** keyword (CR 702.176, introduced in *Duskmourn: House of Horror*) end-to-end in the engine. The keyword is already parsed and typed as `Keyword::Impending(ManaCost)` in `crates/engine/src/types/keywords.rs`, but has no game handling — every Impending card is silently unplayable.

## Rules Reference

> **CR 702.176a:** Impending N—[cost] is a keyword ability. If a player casts a spell for its impending cost, that spell's controller puts N time counters on it as it enters the battlefield. That permanent isn't a creature for as long as it has a time counter on it. At the beginning of that player's end step, they remove a time counter from that permanent.

## Cards Unlocked

All 6 Duskmourn legendaries with Impending:
- Valgavoth, Terror Eater (`Impending 5—{1}{B}`)
- Enduring Tenacity (`Impending 4—{2}{W}{W}`)
- Gloomshrieker (`Impending 4—{1}{U}{U}`)
- Luminary Vex (`Impending 4—{2}{R}{R}`)
- Wandering Wilderness (`Impending 4—{1}{G}{G}`)
- Lurker in the Deep (`Impending 3—{2}{U}{U}`)

## Implementation Scope

**1. Alternate-cost casting** (`game/casting.rs`)
Add `handle_impending` parallel to `handle_foretell` — pay the impending cost at cast time instead of the normal mana cost.

**2. ETB time counter placement**
When a spell was cast for its impending cost, place N time counters on the permanent as it enters the battlefield. N comes from `Keyword::Impending(cost)` on the object.

**3. "Not a creature" continuous effect (Layer 4)**
While the permanent has ≥1 time counter from its impending cast, suppress the creature type. Self-referential continuous effect — follow the Morph/animate pattern for type suppression.

**4. End-step triggered ability**
Synthesize a phase-based trigger: "At the beginning of your end step, remove a time counter from this permanent." Mandatory triggered ability generated from the keyword, similar to Suspend's counter removal.

**5. AI legal actions**
Add impending cast to the candidate generator in `ai_support/candidates.rs`, mirroring the foretell candidate path.

**6. Tests**
- Impending card is castable for its impending cost
- Enters with correct N time counters and is not a creature
- One time counter removed each end step
- Becomes a creature when last counter is removed
- Normal (non-impending) cast still works — enters as a creature with no counters

## Analogs to Follow

| Concern | Existing pattern |
|---|---|
| Alternate cost dispatch | `handle_foretell` in `casting.rs` |
| Casting permission stamp | `CastingPermission::Plotted { turn_plotted }` |
| "Isn't a creature" suppression | Morph / `StaticMode` type-removal effects |
| Phase-based counter removal | Suspend (`TriggerMode::Phase { phase: End }`) |
| AI candidate generation | Foretell block in `candidates.rs` |

## Out of Scope

problems.title: Engine: 'end the turn' (CR 724.1) is unimplemented — Time Stop, Sundial, Obeka, Glorious End, …
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

The "end the turn" effect (CR 724.1) is unimplemented. Cards that end the turn parse their effect clause to `Effect::Unimplemented { name: "end", description: "end the turn" }`, so they don't function.

Affected cards (the "end the turn" effect): **Time Stop, Sundial of the Infinite, Obeka, Brute Chronologist, Glorious End, Discontinuity, Day's Undoing, Hurkyl's Final Meditation, Ultima, The Drum – Mining Facility**.

## What the CR requires

CR 724.1 — when an effect ends the turn, follow these steps (which differ from normal resolution):
- **724.1a:** triggered abilities that triggered before this process began but aren't yet on the stack cease to exist.
- **724.1b:** exile every object on the stack, including the resolving object.
- **724.1c:** check state-based actions; no player gets priority and no triggers are put on the stack.
- **724.1d:** the current phase/step ends; if in combat, remove all creatures and planeswalkers from combat; skip straight to the cleanup step.
- **724.1e:** end-step triggers don't fire (the end step is skipped).

## Steps to reproduce

Resolve Time Stop (or any card above). The effect resolves to `Unimplemented` instead of ending the turn — the stack isn't exiled, combat continues, and the turn proceeds normally.

## Expected behavior

Resolving "end the turn" exiles the stack, removes everything from combat, and jumps to the cleanup step, ending the turn.

## Suggested implementation

A new `Effect::EndTheTurn` (no target) + resolver implementing CR 724.1, and a parser intercept mapping the "end the turn" imperative clause to it.

## Component

- `crates/engine/src/types/ability.rs` (`Effect`/`EffectKind`)
- `crates/engine/src/game/effects/` (resolver) + `crates/engine/src/game/turns.rs` (skip-to-cleanup)
- `crates/engine/src/parser/oracle_effect/imperative.rs` (parser)

problems.title: fix(parser): effect-form "[subject] activated abilities can't be activated" (CR 602.5)
problems.issue_body:
## Summary
Parses the **effect-form** of activation prohibition so cards like **Dovin Baan** (`[+1]: Until your next turn, up to one target creature gets -3/-0 and its activated abilities can't be activated.`), **Xathrid Gorgon**, and **Braided Net** stop falling to the `Effect:its` Unimplemented fallback.

The **static** form (Chalice of Life / Karn — "this permanent's activated abilities can't be activated") already emits `StaticMode::CantBeActivated`. Only the **effect/predicate** form was missing.

## Approach (mirrors the existing `MustBeBlocked` restriction-clause precedent)
- New arm in `try_parse_subject_restriction_clause`: split around `" activated abilities can't be activated"`, resolve the subject, and emit `Effect::GenericEffect { static_abilities: [CantBeActivated { who: AllPlayers, source_filter: SelfRef, exemption }], affected, AddStaticMode }` for `UntilEndOfTurn`. The `CantBeActivated` **resolution already exists** — this only adds the parse path.
- **Subject binding:** possessive-anaphor subjects ("its"/"their"/"that creature's") bind to `TargetFilter::ParentTarget` (the creature targeted by the sibling pump conjunct); typed possessors ("target creature's") strip the trailing `'s` and route through the normal subject grammar.
- **Compound:** one tightly-scoped arm added to the splitter (`starts_bare_and_clause_lower`) so Dovin Baan's `… and its activated abilities can't be activated` conjunct splits and routes to the new arm. The arm matches only the possessive-subject + the exact fixed predicate, so it cannot mis-split unrelated text.

## Files changed
- `crates/engine/src/parser/oracle_effect/subject.rs` — new restriction arm + `subject_application_for_cant_be_activated` helper
- `crates/engine/src/parser/oracle_effect/sequence.rs` — one tightly-scoped compound-splitter arm
- `crates/engine/src/parser/oracle_static/mod.rs` — re-export `parse_cant_be_activated_exemption_in_text` as `pub(crate)`
- `crates/engine/src/parser/oracle_effect/mod.rs` — tests

## Anchored on
- `MustBeBlocked` arm in `try_parse_subject_restriction_clause` (subject.rs:~333) — the exact emission template (parse subject → `static_affected_for_application` → `GenericEffect{mode, AddStaticMode}`).
- `oracle_static/dispatch.rs` (~1143) — the existing `StaticMode::CantBeActivated { who: AllPlayers, source_filter: SelfRef, exemption }` emission whose semantics this mirrors.
- `ParentTarget` anaphor-binding convention used by other targeted restriction clauses.

## CR references
- `CR 602.5` — a player can't begin to activate a prohibited ability
- `CR 603.2a` — activation prohibitions don't affect triggered abilities
- `CR 605.1a` — mana-ability exemption ("unless they're mana abilities")

## Tests
- `cant_be_activated_effect_standalone_targets_creature` — asserts "target creature's activated abilities can't be activated" → `GenericEffect` with `CantBeActivated { who: AllPlayers, source_filter: SelfRef }`, the `AddStaticMode` modification, `affected = ParentTa…

problems.title: Implement Unfinity Attractions subsystem (deck, visit, open, triggers, UI)
problems.issue_body:
## Summary
Ship the full Unfinity Attractions mechanic end-to-end. Today the engine recognizes attraction-related types and parsing fragments, but there is no runtime subsystem: TriggerMode::VisitAttraction is registered with match_unimplemented, “open an Attraction” appears in parser tests without a resolver, and the client has no attraction UI. crates/mtgish-import documents this explicitly as a subsystem-scale deferral that blocks converter output until runtime exists.

## Why

- Attractions are a distinct rules object (separate 10-card deck, visit rolls, prizes, “open” actions) — not a single keyword fix.
- Unlocks a class of Unfinity / acorn-adjacent cards and clears a documented import/converter blocker.
- Good fit for a large, cross-cutting contribution: engine, parser, match flow, frontend, multiplayer visibility, AI, tests.

## Acceptance criteria

- A minimal scripted game can: start with an attraction deck → open an Attraction → complete a visit roll and apply a prize where rules require it.
- At least one real card’s Oracle line for “open an Attraction” resolves without Effect::Unimplemented.
- TriggerMode::VisitAttraction fires on the correct event (not match_unimplemented).
- Frontend shows attraction deck state and supports required player choices.
- Multiplayer game does not leak hidden attraction deck contents to opponents.
- New integration tests pass; existing tests unchanged unless behavior is intentionally fixed.

problems.title: feat: Oathbreaker format support
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

Add Oathbreaker as a playable format. Card legality (`Legality::oathbreaker`)
is already parsed in `mtgjson.rs:104`; this wires it into a full game mode.

## Rules

60-card singleton, one Planeswalker commander + one signature spell
(instant/sorcery within color identity) in the command zone. Signature spell
is only castable while the Oathbreaker is on the battlefield. Both return to
the command zone with independent commander-tax tracking. Starting life: 20.

## Changes

**Rust (`crates/engine/src/`)**
- `types/format.rs` — new `GameFormat::Oathbreaker`, `FormatConfig::oathbreaker()`
- `game/deck_validation.rs` — Planeswalker check, signature spell type/color-identity check
- `game/casting.rs` — signature spell gate (only castable while Oathbreaker is on battlefield)
- `game/commander.rs` — second command-zone slot per player; independent tax counters

**TypeScript (`client/src/`)**
- `data/formatRegistry.ts` — new format entry
- `adapter/types.ts` — extend command zone state to carry `signature_spell_id`
- `pages/GameSetupPage.tsx` — signature spell picker (filtered by color identity)
- `components/board/CommanderDamage.tsx` — second command-zone display slot
- `components/deck-builder/` — validation error messages

**JSON**
- `i18n/locales/*/settings.json` — format name + description (7 languages)
- `i18n/locales/*/deck-builder.json` — validation error keys
- `i18n/locales/*/game.json` — command zone label keys

## Reference

- TinyLeaders analog: `deck_validation.rs:833`, `format.rs:490`
- Commander zone logic: `game/commander.rs`
- Legality field: `database/mtgjson.rs:104`

problems.title: phase-server: in-game GameAction payload lists/strings are unbounded on the wire (no game-action counterpart to draft_ac
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

The native WebSocket server bounds client-supplied payloads for nearly every wire message — including `DraftAction` bodies via `server_core::draft_action_payload_guard` (oversized pick ids / submit-deck lists / match ids). But the in-game `ClientMessage::Action { action: GameAction }` path has **no equivalent guard**: the `GameAction` is handed straight to `mgr.handle_action` (`crates/phase-server/src/main.rs`), and many of its variants carry client-controlled lists/strings that reach clone-heavy engine reducers before legality is fully resolved.

Unbounded client-supplied fields on `GameAction` include:

- `CastSpell` / `CastSpellWithPaymentMode` `targets`, `SelectTargets` `targets`, `RetargetSpell` `new_targets`
- `DeclareAttackers` `attacks`, `DeclareBlockers` `assignments`, `AssignCombatDamage` `assignments`, `DistributeAmong` `distribution`
- `SelectCards` `cards`, `ReorderHand` / `OrderTriggers` `order`, `SelectCoinFlips` `keep_indices`, `SelectModes` `indices`
- `CrewVehicle` / `SaddleMount` `creature_ids`, `SubmitSideboard` `main`/`sideboard`, `SubmitPilePartition` `pile_a`, `SetPhaseStops` `stops`, `ChooseOutsideGameCards` / `ChooseCounterMoveDistribution` `selections`
- `ChooseOption` `choice` (free-form string)

## Impact

A client can send a `GameAction` with a multi-thousand- (or million-) entry list, forcing large allocations/clones in the reducers before the action is rejected — the same clone-heavy-reducer concern that motivated `draft_action_payload_guard`, but on the main game path.

## Steps to reproduce

Connect to a native `phase-server`, join a game, and send an `Action` whose `GameAction` carries an enormous list (e.g. `ReorderHand { order: <100k ObjectIds> }`). The payload reaches the reducer unbounded.

## Expected behavior

Client-supplied `GameAction` list/string sizes are bounded at the wire boundary before dispatch, mirroring `draft_action_payload_guard`. The bound must be generous enough never to reject legitimate play (including large token-board states) — it only blocks adversarial payloads.

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/game_action_payload_guard.rs` (new)
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` — the `ClientMessage::Action` handler.

problems.title: [Bug]: "can't attack alone" / "can't block alone" is doubly unimplemented — the restriction is never parsed and never en
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

Creatures that **"can't attack alone"**, **"can't block alone"**, or **"can't attack or block alone"** (Mogg Flunkies, Bonded Construct, Ember Beast, Jackal Familiar, …) ignore the restriction entirely. There are two independent gaps:

1. **Parser** — the Oracle static parser has **no branch** for "can't attack alone" / "can't block alone", so the clause is silently dropped; no static is produced.
2. **Enforcement** — even though `StaticMode::CantAttackAlone` and `StaticMode::CantBlockAlone` exist and are registered, **combat never consults them**. `validate_attackers` and `validate_blockers` contain no "alone" check, so the statics are dead.

The result: a creature that "can't attack alone" may attack as the only attacker, and one that "can't block alone" may block as the only blocker — both illegal under the Comprehensive Rules.

## Cards (a class, not a one-off)

The source data carries `CantAttackAlone` (14 cards) and `CantBlockAlone` (8 cards):

- **Mogg Flunkies** — "Mogg Flunkies can't attack or block alone." (both)
- **Bonded Construct** — "can't attack alone."
- **Ember Beast**, **Jackal Familiar**, **Bonded Horncrest**, **Loyal Pegasus**, **Sightless Brawler**, **Raging Kronch**, **Pipsqueak, Rebel Strongarm**, **Militia Rallier**, … — "can't attack alone" / "can't attack or block alone."

These are mostly aggressive commons with a downside that's currently free.

## Expected

Per **CR 506.5**, a creature "attacks alone" if it's the only creature declared as an attacker, and "blocks alone" if it's the only creature declared as a blocker.

- A creature with "can't attack alone" may be declared as an attacker **only if at least one other creature is also declared as an attacker** (CR 508.1a — a declaration disobeying a restriction is illegal). Two creatures that each "can't attack alone" may legally attack together (CR 506.5 Example).
- A creature with "can't block alone" may be declared as a blocker **only if at least one other creature is also declared as a blocker** (CR 509.1b).
- "can't attack or block alone" imposes both.

## Actual

The Oracle clause never parses into a static, and combat declaration applies no "alone" constraint. The creature attacks/blocks alone freely — the printed downside does nothing.

## Root cause

### 1. No parser branch

`crates/engine/src/parser/oracle_static/` — a search for "attack alone" / "block alone" / `CantAttackAlone` / `CantBlockAlone` across `crates/engine/src/parser` returns **no production code**. The phrasing isn't recognized, so `parse_static_line` never emits `StaticMode::CantAttackAlone` / `CantBlockAlone`. (Contrast the sibling combat restrictions "can't attack" / "can't block", which are parsed.)

### 2. No combat enforcement

`crates/engine/src/game/combat.rs`:

- `validate_attackers` (line 351) checks attack legality but has no `CantAttackAlone` clause.
- `validate_blockers` / `validate_blockers_for_player` (line 521+) enforce menace minimums, `MustBlock`, `MustBeBlocked`, …

problems.title: [Bug]: SpectatorJoin has no per-game spectator cap
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: SpectatorJoin has no per-game spectator cap

## Description

`SpectatorJoin` bounds `game_code` via `guard_spectator_join`, but after #1884 live spectator fan-out there is no limit on how many connections can register as spectators for one game. Each join clones a full privacy-filtered game snapshot (`build_spectator_game_started_message`) and stores another `mpsc` sender in `game_spectators`.

An attacker can open many WebSockets, send valid `SpectatorJoin` frames for the same `game_code`, and force repeated large state snapshots and unbounded growth of the spectator list.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Start a live game on native `phase-server` Full mode.
2. Open N WebSocket clients; each sends `ClientHello` then `SpectatorJoin { game_code }` for the same code.
3. Observe N entries in `game_spectators` and N snapshot builds with no cap.

## Expected behavior

Wire or session policy caps spectators per game (and/or rate-limits join) before snapshot fan-out.

## Actual behavior

Unbounded spectator registrations per `game_code`.

## Impact

Memory and CPU amplification; broadcast work scales with attacker connection count (DoS).

## Component

- `crates/server-core/src/spectator_wire_guard.rs` (extend) or new guard
- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (`SpectatorJoin` handler)

problems.title: [Bug]: Full-mode create/join clones deck before inbound guard
problems.issue_body:
# [Bug]: Full-mode create/join clones deck before inbound guard

## Description

LobbyOnly `CreateGameWithSettings` / `JoinGameWithPassword` call `lobby_broker::validate_deck_payload` on the deserialized `deck` before broker dispatch. Full-mode handlers build a projected `LobbyClientMessage` with `deck: deck.clone()` and only then call `lobby_broker::guard_inbound`, so oversized card lists are fully duplicated even when validation would reject them on the first field check.

`JoinGameWithPassword` in Full mode also runs `reject_joining_current_game` before `guard_inbound`, so unbounded `game_code` / `display_name` strings can be compared and cloned ahead of wire rejection (join path).

## Steps to reproduce

1. Connect to native `phase-server` in **Full** mode.
2. Send `ClientHello`, then `CreateGameWithSettings` with `main_deck` containing 501+ card name strings (valid JSON otherwise).
3. Observe the server clones the entire deck into the `guard_inbound` projection before returning an error.

Repeat with `JoinGameWithPassword` and the same oversized deck.

## Expected behavior

Full-mode create/join validate deck (and other bounded lobby fields) on the deserialized frame **before** cloning into `LobbyClientMessage` for `guard_inbound`.

## Actual behavior

`deck.clone()` (and other `.clone()` calls on the projection) run before rejection.

## Impact

Memory and CPU amplification on Full-mode session create/join (DoS).

## Component

- `crates/phase-server/src/main.rs` (Full-mode `CreateGameWithSettings`, `JoinGameWithPassword`)
- Optionally `crates/server-core/src/` early-validation helper shared with LobbyOnly path

problems.title: [Bug]: "as though it didn't have hexproof" (Detection Tower class) does nothing — `StaticMode::IgnoreHexproof` is never
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

Effects that let a player target hexproof permanents **"as though they didn't have hexproof"** — the primary answers to hexproof commanders — have no effect. `StaticMode::IgnoreHexproof` exists and is registered, and `can_target` even carries a comment claiming "*CR 702.11e: IgnoreHexproof bypasses this*", but:

1. **Targeting never checks it.** `can_target` consults the `Shroud` / `Hexproof` / `HexproofFrom` keywords and protection — but there is no `IgnoreHexproof` check anywhere. The comment is aspirational; the code does not implement it.
2. **The parser never produces it.** No code in `crates/engine/src/parser` emits `StaticMode::IgnoreHexproof`; the Oracle phrase "as though it/they didn't have hexproof" isn't recognized, so even the parse is missing.

`IgnoreHexproof` is a doubly-dead static: a defined, registered, `Display`/`FromStr`-round-tripping variant that nothing creates and nothing reads. As a result, **Detection Tower** and similar hexproof-bypass effects are inert — a hexproof creature remains an illegal target even after the controller activates the bypass.

## Cards (a played, high-impact class)

- **Detection Tower** — "{1}, {T}: Until end of turn, your opponents and creatures they control with hexproof can be the targets of spells and abilities you control **as though they didn't have hexproof**." A Commander staple — one of the few colorless answers to hexproof generals.
- Other "target … as though it didn't have hexproof" effects in the same class.
- **Related dead static (same hexproof-answer area):** `StaticMode::CantHaveKeyword` is likewise never consumed in `crates/engine/src/game`, so the "**can't have or be granted hexproof or shroud**" half of **Arcane Lighthouse** is also unimplemented. (Arcane Lighthouse's "lose hexproof and shroud" half works via `RemoveKeyword`; the "can't have/be granted" half does not.)

## Expected

While the bypass effect is active, the controller's spells and abilities may target the named hexproof permanents (CR 702.11e — an effect may allow targeting "as though" the permanent didn't have hexproof). Other players' spells/abilities are unaffected.

## Actual

The bypass clause is dropped at parse time, and `can_target` continues to treat the permanent as hexproof. The hexproof permanent stays an illegal target for everyone, so Detection Tower does nothing.

## Root cause

### 1. `can_target` never checks `IgnoreHexproof`

`crates/engine/src/game/targeting.rs:1322-1356` — `can_target` returns `false` for `Hexproof` (when `obj.controller != source_controller`) and `HexproofFrom`, with a comment at line 1338 referencing `IgnoreHexproof` — but no code path consults it. A whole-crate search finds `StaticMode::IgnoreHexproof` only in: the enum definition, the registry insert (`handle_rule_mod`, inert), that lone comment, and a `Display`/`FromStr` round-trip test. It is never compared or queried for targeting.

### 2. The parser never produces it

A search of `crates/engine/src/parser` f…

problems.title: Engine: 'end the combat phase' (CR 724.2) is unimplemented — Mandate of Peace
problems.issue_body:
## Summary

The "end the combat phase" effect (CR 724.2) is unimplemented. Mandate of Peace parses its `End the combat phase.` clause to `Effect::Unimplemented`, so it doesn't function. This is the combat-phase counterpart to "end the turn" (CR 724.1), implemented in #1909.

## Affected card

**Mandate of Peace** — "Cast this spell only during combat. Your opponents can't cast spells this turn. End the combat phase." Per CR 724.2, this is currently the only card with the effect.

## What the CR requires

CR 724.2 — when an effect ends the combat phase, follow these steps (which differ from normal resolution):
- **724.2a:** triggered abilities that triggered before this process began but aren't yet on the stack cease to exist.
- **724.2b:** exile every object on the stack, including the resolving object.
- **724.2c:** check state-based actions; no player gets priority and no triggers are put on the stack.
- **724.2d:** the current combat phase ends — remove all creatures and planeswalkers from combat, "until end of combat" effects expire, and the game skips straight to the next phase (usually the postcombat main phase).
- **724.2e:** "at end of combat" triggers don't fire (the end-of-combat step is skipped).
- **724.2g:** if it isn't a combat phase, nothing happens.

## Steps to reproduce

Resolve Mandate of Peace during combat. The effect resolves to `Unimplemented` instead of ending the combat phase — the stack isn't exiled and combat continues into the next combat step normally.

## Expected behavior

Resolving "end the combat phase" exiles the stack, removes everything from combat, expires "until end of combat" effects, and jumps to the postcombat main phase.

## Suggested implementation

A new `Effect::EndCombatPhase` (no target) + resolver implementing CR 724.2 (a sibling of `Effect::EndTheTurn`), and a parser intercept mapping the "end the combat phase" imperative clause to it.

## Component

- `crates/engine/src/types/ability.rs` (`Effect`/`EffectKind`)
- `crates/engine/src/game/effects/` (resolver) + `crates/engine/src/game/turns.rs` (skip-to-postcombat-main)
- `crates/engine/src/parser/oracle_effect/imperative.rs` (parser)

problems.title: Implement Specialize keyword runtime (choose color + transform)
problems.issue_body:
## Description:

Card data already supports CardLayout::Specialize and variant faces, but the engine does not implement the mechanic.

## Current gap

- Keyword::Specialize is a type-only stub (no alt-cast, color choice, or face swap)
- TriggerMode::Specializes is not wired to events

Goal Support Strixhaven-style specialize: pay the specialize cost, choose a color, and transform the permanent to the matching specialized face.

## Likely work

- Casting / alt-cast flow
- Object face switching
- Trigger matcher for “when ~ specializes”
- Parser/coverage updates
- Frontend face display
- AI legal actions for specialize timing

problems.title: [Bug]: Predefined **Junk**, **Incubator**, and **Shard** tokens spawn without their printed activated abilities
problems.issue_body:
# Summary

`predefined_token_abilities` in `crates/engine/src/game/effects/token.rs` returns abilities for Food, Clue, Treasure, etc., but explicitly leaves **Junk**, **Incubator** (transform), and **Shard** as a TODO (`_ => vec![]`). Many cards create `Junk` tokens (Fallout / Universes Beyond); `known-tokens.toml` already defines Junk rules text, yet created tokens have **no** `{T}, Sacrifice…` ability on the battlefield.

# Impact

- **Cards broken:** Break Down, Boomer Scrapper, Crimson Caravaneer, Junktown, and other `CreatableToken::Junk` sources — tokens are inert artifacts.
- **Broader:** Incubator / Shard predefined tokens (Phyrexian / etc.) lack transform or sacrifice abilities until implemented.

# Steps to reproduce

1. In a test game or scenario, resolve an effect that creates a **Junk** token (e.g. **Break Down** destroying an artifact/enchantment).
2. Select the Junk token on the battlefield.
3. Attempt to activate `{T}, Sacrifice this artifact: Exile the top card…` (sorcery speed).

# Expected behavior

The token has the predefined activated ability from CR 111.10 / oracle text in `known-tokens.toml` (`rules_text` for subtype `Junk`), activatable at sorcery speed.

# Actual behavior

`predefined_token_abilities("Junk")` returns an empty vector; the token has no activated abilities. Same for Incubator/Shard until the TODO is implemented.

# Additional information

- **Suggested fix:** Add `junk_ability()`, incubator transform handling, and shard ability helpers; keep `predefined_token_abilities` and `predefined_token_rules_text` in sync (file comment requires paired edits).

problems.title: `prepare_body` stream detection regression
problems.issue_body:
<!-- Summary. -->
Requests 2.34.0 changed the stream detection in PreparedRequest.prepare_body() from `hasattr(data, "__iter__")` to `isinstance(data, Iterable)`. This breaks file-like wrappers that expose `__iter__/read/tell/seek` via `__getattr__ delegation` (e.g. tqdm.wrapattr), because `isinstance(..., Iterable)` only checks the class MRO, not dynamic attribute delegation. As a result, `_body_position` is never recorded and the body cannot be rewound on 307/308 redirects.
## Expected Result

Upload completes successfully through the redirect, as it did in 2.33.1.

## Actual Result

The redirected request sends an empty/consumed body. The server receives no data and the request times out:
```
requests.exceptions.ReadTimeout: HTTPSConnectionPool(...): Read timed out. (read timeout=30)
```

## Reproduction Steps

```python
import os
import requests
from tqdm import tqdm

with open("testfile.txt", "wb") as f:
    f.write(b"hello world " * 1000)

with open("testfile.txt", "rb") as f:
    with tqdm.wrapattr(f, "read", total=os.path.getsize("testfile.txt")) as wrapped:
        # Any endpoint that 307 redirects to a presigned S3 URL
        requests.put("https://your-api.example.com/upload", data=wrapped, timeout=30)

```

## System Information

    $ python -m requests.help

```json
{
  "chardet": {
    "version": null
  },
  "charset_normalizer": {
    "version": "3.4.7"
  },

    "version": ""

    "version": "3.13"
  },

    "name": "CPython",
    "version": "3.13.5"
  },

    "release": "24.6.0",
    "system": "Darwin"
  },
  "pyOpenSSL": {
    "openssl_version": "",
    "version": null
  },

    "version": "2.34.0"
  },

    "version": "30000120"
  },

    "version": "2.5.0"

  "using_charset_normalizer": true,
  "using_pyopenssl": false
}
```

please provide some basic information about your system (Python version,

problems.title: Inherit fixture from baseclass with class scope fails
problems.issue_body:
I have an inherited fixture from a baseclass. From searching this can work [63292928](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/63292928/add-pytest-fixtures-to-a-test-class-using-dependency-injection).
However my example uses a class scope which does then not work.
The provided example will fail both tests. When removing the scope of the fixture both tests will pass.


```
import pytest


class Base:
    name = None
    variable = None

    def setup(self):
        self.variable = self.name
        print(f"setup: {self.variable}")

    def teardown(self):
        print(f"teardown: {self.variable}")

    @pytest.fixture(scope="class")
    def fix(self):
        try:
            self.setup()
            yield
        finally:
            self.teardown()


@pytest.mark.usefixtures("fix")
class Test1(Base):
    name = "test1"

    def test_a(self):
        assert self.variable == self.name


@pytest.mark.usefixtures("fix")
class Test2(Base):
    name = "test2"

    def test_a(self):
        assert self.variable == self.name
```
