  -> strong context: summarize/suggest directly
  -> weak context: mark OCR fallback needed
```

Milestone 3 can implement the interface/detection first. OCR execution can be added when the local OCR runtime is selected.

## User workflow

When TouchPilot cannot understand enough from Accessibility data, it should respond honestly:

```text
I can see this screen, but the app exposes limited readable UI data. OCR support is needed for better understanding here.
```

## Local-first impact

This keeps the primary path local and deterministic while preparing for future local OCR/VLM support.

## Safety and security considerations

- OCR output should be treated as untrusted and noisy.
- OCR text may include sensitive content and must go through redaction before logging.
- Weak context should not cause unsafe guessing or blind tapping.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add context quality or weak-tree detection logic.
- [ ] Define an OCR fallback interface/boundary or placeholder result type.
- [ ] Mark local VLM as future fallback, not a Milestone 3 dependency.
- [ ] Weak context produces conservative summary/suggestion behavior.
- [ ] Tests cover strong context, weak context, and empty context.
- [ ] Documentation or comments explain that OCR/VLM fallback remains local-only.

## References

- Parent issue: #38
- `docs/LOCAL_INFERENCE.md`
- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`

problems.title: feature: define screen context model
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #38

## Problem

Milestone 3 needs a stable screen context model before TouchPilot can summarize screens, suggest actions, or feed useful local context into the reasoning core and future local models.

Current Android observation data is useful for debugging and tool execution, but it needs a normalized product-facing shape that is safe, testable, and independent from raw Accessibility implementation details.

## Proposed behavior

Define a structured screen context model that represents the current Android screen with enough information for local reasoning:

- current app label and package name when available
- screen/window title when available
- visible text
- clickable nodes
- input fields
- scrollable containers or scroll state
- node bounds
- enabled/focused/checked/clickable state
- stable node identifiers when available
- redaction-aware text fields

The model should be usable by:

- deterministic screen summaries
- suggested actions
- local model input contracts
- trace/log export
- future OCR/VLM fallback layers

## User workflow

When TouchPilot observes a screen, the runtime should be able to convert raw screen data into a stable context object before any summary or action suggestion is generated.

Example flow:

```text
Accessibility tree
  -> ScreenContext
  -> summary / suggested actions / local model input
```

## Local-first impact

A stable screen context model keeps screen understanding local and reusable. It lets TouchPilot improve local models later without changing the Android control layer or UI.

## Safety and security considerations

- Screen context may include sensitive text.
- Sensitive text fields should be marked or redacted before logs/traces.
- Raw Accessibility data should not be exposed directly to user-visible summaries without filtering.
- The model should support policy decisions later, such as sensitive app or sensitive field detection.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add a stable screen context data model.
- [ ] Model includes app/package, visible text, clickable nodes, input fields, bounds, and state flags where available.
- [ ] Model distinguishes raw text from redaction-safe display/log text where practical.
- [ ] Model is independent from UI components.
- [ ] Model can be used as input to local reasoning/model contracts.
- [ ] Unit tests cover construction of representative screen context objects.
- [ ] Documentation or code comments explain the role of the model boundary.

## References

- Parent issue: #38
- `docs/ROADMAP.md` Milestone 3
- `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`
- `docs/TOOL_SPEC.md`

problems.title: feature: build screen context from accessibility tree
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #38

## Problem

After defining the screen context model, TouchPilot needs a reliable builder that converts Android Accessibility observations into that model.

Raw Accessibility trees are noisy and inconsistent. The builder should normalize data into a smaller, useful context for local reasoning and suggested actions.

## Proposed behavior

Implement an Accessibility-to-screen-context builder:

```text
Accessibility observation
  -> normalize nodes
  -> extract visible text
  -> extract clickable targets
  -> extract input fields
  -> extract bounds/state
  -> produce ScreenContext
```

The builder should keep raw observation details available for debugging, but the reasoning core should use the normalized context.

## User workflow

When the user asks `What can you do here?`, TouchPilot should first build screen context from the active Android screen and then generate a local summary or suggestions from that context.

## Local-first impact

This is the primary screen-understanding path. It avoids requiring screenshot vision models for normal Android screens.

## Safety and security considerations

- Builder output should remain compatible with log redaction.
- Sensitive fields such as passwords, tokens, messages, and account data should be marked or redacted where possible.
- The builder should not create executable actions directly; it should only produce context.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Build screen context from current Accessibility observation data.
- [ ] Extract visible text nodes.
- [ ] Extract clickable targets with bounds and stable identifiers when available.
- [ ] Extract input fields and basic state.
- [ ] Extract scrollable state where available.
- [ ] Keep raw observation and normalized context separate.
- [ ] Add tests with fake/sample Accessibility data.
- [ ] Existing observe/tool behavior remains compatible.

## References

- Parent issue: #38
- Depends on: screen context model issue
- `docs/TOOL_SPEC.md`
- `docs/LIVE_TESTING.md`

problems.title: feature: hybrid local AI core (Milestone 2)
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

Milestone 1 proved the local-first foundation, but TouchPilot still behaves mostly like a deterministic command router with a local model runtime path. Milestone 2 should turn the foundation into a real 100% local hybrid AI core.

The goal is not to make one small local model do everything. The goal is to combine deterministic routing, local model reasoning, skills, safety policy, and agent events into one clean runtime boundary.

## Proposed behavior

Implement the Hybrid Local AI Core:

```text
user message
    │
    ▼
┌────────────┐   ┌────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐
│ Intent     │ → │ Reasoning  │ → │ Agent Event  │
│ Gate       │   │ Core       │   │ Stream       │
└────────────┘   └─────┬──────┘   └──────┬───────┘
                       │                 │
      exact commands ◄─┼─► local model   ▼
      skill match      │          tool running / success /
      safety precheck  │          failure / approval / final
                       ▼
              deterministic fallback
```

Milestone 2 is complete when TouchPilot can route simple requests deterministically, use a local model path for ambiguous requests, and expose all work as local agent events.

## User workflow

A user sends a request such as:

- `Hello`
- `Help`
- `Open Settings`
- `Go back`
- `Open Wi-Fi settings`
- `What can you do on this screen?`

TouchPilot should:

1. Build local request context.
2. Run an intent gate.
3. Choose one of:
   - exact deterministic command
   - known skill match
   - local model reasoning path
   - clarification / blocked response
4. Emit structured agent events for the UI.
5. Route any tool request through validation, skill allowlists, and policy.
6. Return a final response or visible tool result.

## Required features and fixes

- Define a local reasoning core boundary separate from UI and Android tool execution.
- Add an intent gate for exact commands, known skills, unsafe requests, and model-needed requests.
- Remove cloud/provider fallback from the normal product path.
- Define local model input/output contracts for:
  - intent classification
  - tool selection
  - argument extraction
  - target ranking
  - final answer
- Build shared agent event types for:
  - user message
  - assistant message
  - tool requested
  - tool running
  - tool succeeded
  - tool failed
  - approval required
  - blocked by policy
  - final answer
- Keep deterministic router as fallback and test baseline.
- Ensure local model outputs are treated as untrusted and still pass through tool validation, skill allowlists, policy, and logging.
- Add offline-only validation for the main Milestone 2 flows.
- Add focused tests for routing decisions and event emission.

## Local-first impact

This milestone removes cloud/provider thinking from the core product path and makes TouchPilot's intelligence local by design:

```text
local deterministic router
  + local model reasoning
  + local skills
  + local policy
  + local logs
  = hybrid local Android AI core
``…

problems.title: feature: add local screen summary and suggested actions
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #38

## Problem

TouchPilot needs a visible local AI feature from screen context: it should be able to summarize the current screen and suggest useful actions without cloud inference.

This should start deterministic and context-driven. A local model hook can be added later, but Milestone 3 should not depend on a VLM or cloud model.

## Proposed behavior

Add local screen summary and suggested action generation from `ScreenContext`:

```text
ScreenContext
  -> screen summary
  -> suggested actions
  -> agent/UI event output
```

Example output:

```text
I see the Settings screen. Visible actions include Network & internet, Connected devices, Apps, Notifications, and Battery. I can open one of these sections, scroll, or go back.
```

Suggested actions should map to existing safe tool concepts where possible:

- tap visible item
- type into visible input
- scroll
- go back
- open app-specific visible section

## User workflow

User asks:

- `What screen am I on?`
- `What can you do here?`
- `What buttons are visible?`
- `Suggest actions for this screen`

TouchPilot replies locally using the current screen context.

## Local-first impact

This makes TouchPilot feel like a real local AI assistant while still using deterministic, inspectable local logic first.

## Safety and security considerations

- Summaries should avoid exposing sensitive details from password fields, private messages, tokens, or account screens.
- Suggested actions are not execution approval. Any selected action must still go through validation and policy.
- Suggestions should be conservative on sensitive screens.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add local screen summary generation from screen context.
- [ ] Add suggested action generation from visible controls.
- [ ] Suggested actions reference existing tool concepts without executing automatically.
- [ ] UI or agent event path can display the summary and suggestions.
- [ ] Sensitive text is redacted or avoided in summaries where practical.
- [ ] Tests cover Settings-like screen, launcher-like screen, input-field screen, and empty/weak context.
- [ ] No cloud model/backend is required.

## References

- Parent issue: #38
- Depends on: screen context model and builder issues
- `docs/ROADMAP.md` Milestone 3

problems.title: test: add offline screen understanding validation
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #38

## Problem

Milestone 3 should not be considered complete until screen understanding works offline on representative screens and produces expected summaries, suggestions, and redacted logs.

## Proposed behavior

Add Milestone 3 offline validation covering screen context and understanding:

- TouchPilot screen
- Android Settings screen
- Launcher screen
- input-field screen
- weak/limited Accessibility screen if available

Validation should confirm:

- screen context is built locally
- summary is generated locally
- suggested actions are generated locally
- no cloud/backend is required
- sensitive text remains redacted in logs/traces

## User workflow

A tester should be able to run TouchPilot on emulator or real device, open common screens, ask what TouchPilot sees, and verify that the app responds with local summaries and possible actions.

## Local-first impact

This proves Milestone 3's core claim: TouchPilot can understand Android screens locally without relying on cloud vision/chat models.

## Safety and security considerations

- Include at least one text-entry or sensitive-looking screen in validation to confirm redaction behavior.
- Suggested actions should not execute automatically during validation unless explicitly selected.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add a Milestone 3 offline validation checklist.
- [ ] Validate TouchPilot screen context.
- [ ] Validate Android Settings screen context.
- [ ] Validate launcher screen context.
- [ ] Validate an input-field screen.
- [ ] Validate weak/limited Accessibility behavior where practical.
- [ ] Confirm summaries and suggestions require no cloud/backend.
- [ ] Confirm logs/traces redact sensitive text where applicable.
- [ ] Add automated tests where practical and document manual live-test steps.

## References

- Parent issue: #38
- `docs/LIVE_TESTING.md`
- `docs/ROADMAP.md` Milestone 3

problems.title: Bug: Tools, MCP, and Logs tabs share a single `outputText` field, leaking tool results into unrelated section cards
problems.issue_body:
### What happened?

The MCP tab's "MCP result" card and the Logs tab's "Latest result" card display the output of the *most recent operation from any of the three tabs*, not the most recent operation from that tab.

The cause is a single `private var outputText: String` on `MainActivity` ([MainActivity.kt:733](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L733)):

```kotlin
private var outputText: String = "No result yet."
```

This same field is written from three places:

- `executeAndRender` on the **Tools** tab ([MainActivity.kt:737](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L737))
- Every **MCP** button click handler — List Tools, Call Tool, success and failure branches ([MainActivity.kt:640](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L640), [L664](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L664), [L677](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L677), [L688](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L688))
- The **Logs** tab's "Export Debug Trace" button ([MainActivity.kt:707](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L707))

…and read from three different cards:

- Tools — `timelineCard("Latest result", outputText)` ([MainActivity.kt:616](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L616))
- MCP — `timelineCard("MCP result", outputText)` ([MainActivity.kt:699](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L699))
- Logs — `timelineCard("Latest result", outputText)` ([MainActivity.kt:711](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/MainActivity.kt#L711))

Because every `showSection(...)` call wipes the view tree and re-runs the render function, the card always shows the **current global** `outputText`, regardless of which tab produced it.

Concrete leak directions observed on the running build:

1. **Tools → MCP / Logs**: Tapping `Observe Current Screen` on the Tools tab stores the snapshot result in `outputText`. Switching to the MCP tab shows `MCP result: observe_screen({}) -> false: TouchPilot Control is not enabled.`, and Logs shows the same string under `Latest result`. The user has no way to tell that the message originated in Tools and is unrelated to MCP.
2. **MCP → Tools / Logs**: Tapping `Call Tool` against an unreachable endpoint produces `MCP call failed: failed to connect to /10.0.2.2 (port 8000) ...`. Switching to Tools shows that same MCP-flavoured error under `Latest result`, suggesting the most recent tool execution failed when no tool was executed.
3. **Logs → Tools / MCP**: Tapping `Export Debug Trace` writes the export path into `outputText`. Switching to MCP shows `Debug trace exported: /storage/.../touchpilot-trace-...txt` under `MCP result`, which is nonsensical in MCP context.

The `ToolExecutionLog` shown lower on the Logs tab is correctly siloed — only the "Latest result" card leaks — which makes the bug particularly confusing: the user sees one card claiming `observe_screen(...)` was just run, while the audit-log card below correctly sho…

problems.title: Bug: `SensitiveTextRedactor` mangles `Authorization: Bearer` headers and its greedy token regex eats surrounding structu
problems.issue_body:
### What happened?

[`SensitiveTextRedactor.redact(text: String)`](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/security/SensitiveTextRedactor.kt#L15-L27) contains **two distinct bugs** that fire together whenever an `Authorization: Bearer <token>` substring is redacted as part of a larger log line — which is the exact case for tool-execution log strings written by `executeAndRender` and surfaced on the Tools tab and in `Export Debug Trace`.

### Bug A — `Authorization` header structure is destroyed

The second assignment pattern uses `(?i)(authorization:\s*bearer\s+)[^\s,;]+` and shares the same replacement lambda as the first pattern:

```kotlin
redacted = pattern.replace(redacted) { match ->
    val prefix = match.value.substringBeforeAny(listOf(":", "="))
    "$prefix=[REDACTED]"
}
```

For input `Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz`:

- `match.value` = `Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz`
- `substringBeforeAny(listOf(":", "="))` returns `Authorization` (split at the first `:`)
- replacement = `Authorization=[REDACTED]`

The output is `Authorization=[REDACTED]`. The `Bearer` keyword is dropped, and the HTTP-header `:` is rewritten to an assignment `=`. The first capture group (`(authorization:\s*bearer\s+)`) is captured but never used — the regex is written as if a `"$1[REDACTED]"`-style replacement were intended, and that is presumably the original intent.

### Bug B — the greedy `[^\s,;]+` token consumes trailing `}`, `)`, `]`, `>` etc.

`[^\s,;]+` is greedy and only excludes whitespace, comma, and semicolon. Closing brackets, parentheses, angle brackets, quotes, and any other structural character get eaten as part of the "token" and replaced along with the secret.

Concretely, the tool-execution log string produced by `MainActivity.executeAndRender` for a `type_text` call with `text=Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz` is:

```
type_text({text=Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz}) -> false: typeIntoFocusedField
```

After redaction, the actual output is:

```
type_text({text=Authorization=[REDACTED] -> false: typeIntoFocusedField
```

The `})` that closes the args dict and the tool-call parenthesis is **gone** — the regex matched `Authorization: Bearer abc123xyz})` (eating the closing `})`) and replaced the whole match with `Authorization=[REDACTED]`, throwing the brackets away.

Same class of structural damage happens with:

- `password=secret123}` inside an args dict — `password=[REDACTED]`, closing `}` deleted
- `api_key=sk-abc.def\nsomething` — the regex would also eat through anything not whitespace/comma/semicolon, although newlines do happen to count as whitespace so this particular case stops at end-of-line
- any JSON-ish args printout where the secret is the last value before `}` or `)`

The mangling is visible in the Tools tab's "Latest result" card after the [bug2 fix](touchpilot/bug2.md) (or in the unified card on `main`), in approval cards rendered by `buildApprovalMessage`, and in trace files written by `exportDebugTrace`.

The audit-log section on…

problems.title: Bug: `DefaultActionPolicy.blockedWorkflow` substring-matches against the active screen text, blocking unrelated benign a
problems.issue_body:
### What happened?

[`DefaultActionPolicy.evaluate`](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/security/ActionPolicy.kt#L54-L67) builds a `haystack` string that concatenates the tool name, the argument values, **and the entire `request.activeScreen` snapshot**, then runs [`blockedWorkflow`](app/src/main/java/dev/touchpilot/app/security/ActionPolicy.kt#L109-L131) which checks whether any of these substrings appear anywhere in the haystack:

```
payment, "pay ", password, passcode, account recovery, recover account,
factory reset, erase all, delete account, purchase, buy now, bank,
wire transfer, transfer money
```

Because `activeScreen` is the raw accessibility-tree text of whatever app the user is currently looking at, the policy returns `PolicyDecision.Block` **for every non-LOW-risk tool** the moment the foreground screen contains any of those tokens — even when the tool, its arguments, and the user's actual intent have nothing to do with the supposedly-blocked workflow.

The check runs before the message-send detector, before the MCP approval branch, and before the regular HIGH/MEDIUM approval branch (lines 67-91), so once a keyword appears on screen the agent loses its ability to take **any** medium- or high-risk action: `tap`, `open_app`, `type_text`, `scroll`, `press_back`, `press_home`, and any other MEDIUM/HIGH tool added in the future.

### Concrete examples that fire today

Every one of the following is a benign action whose arguments and tool name contain none of the blocked keywords, but the policy blocks it because the foreground screen contains an unrelated keyword:

| Tool | Args | Foreground screen text contains | Returned decision |
|------|------|--------------------------------|-------------------|
| `tap` | `text=Settings` | `"Banking app home screen"` | `Block("banking or financial actions are blocked")` |
| `open_app` | `target=Calculator` | `"Passwords saved earlier in this session"` | `Block("password workflows are blocked")` |
| `scroll` | `direction=forward` | `"Order history including past purchase totals"` | `Block("purchases are blocked")` |
| `press_back` | (none) | `"Help article: how to delete account permanently"` | `Block("destructive account changes are blocked")` |

Real-world consequences in normal Android usage:

- Browsing a news article that mentions "factory reset" anywhere on screen — every tap is blocked.
- Sitting on the Android system Settings → Passwords & accounts screen — every action is blocked, even pressing Home to leave the screen.
- Notification shade pulls in any banking, payment, or shopping notification text — every action is blocked until the notification is dismissed.
- A skill explicitly designed to navigate a banking app's read-only screens — entirely unable to act.

Crucially, the check provides **no actual security**: an attacker (or buggy model output) can perform the exact action the keyword is meant to discourage as long as the *foreground app currently on screen* does not happen to dis…

problems.title: feature: add Android target selector model
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

TouchPilot tools currently pass target data as loose arguments such as `text`, `node_id`, or `bounds`. That makes tool behavior harder to validate, rank, debug, and extend.

## Proposed behavior

Add a stable target selector model for Android UI targets.

The selector should support:

- `text`
- `contentDescription`
- `nodeId`
- `bounds`
- `viewIdResourceName`
- optional role/package/window metadata
- confidence/source metadata for resolver output

## User workflow

When the agent or local router chooses a UI target, TouchPilot should represent the target in one structured shape before execution.

## Local-first impact

A local agent needs deterministic, inspectable target data. This selector model becomes the contract between screen understanding, routing, and Android tool execution.

## Safety and security considerations

Selectors must be redacted in logs when they contain sensitive text. The model should make ambiguity explicit rather than encouraging blind taps.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add a structured selector model for Android targets.
- [ ] Support current `tap` selectors: text, node id, and bounds.
- [ ] Include view id/content description fields for future resolver work.
- [ ] Add unit tests for selector construction and redaction-sensitive fields where practical.
- [ ] Do not change runtime tool behavior in this PR unless necessary.

## Alternatives considered

Keep loose string maps. Rejected because Milestone 4 needs stronger validation, ranking, and verification contracts.

problems.title: feature: add focus_input tool
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

`type_text` currently depends on whichever field is already focused. That is unreliable for forms, search boxes, and settings screens where the intended field is visible but not focused.

## Proposed behavior

Add a `focus_input` Android tool that focuses a resolved editable target without typing text.

Expected arguments should support the Milestone 4 selector model, such as:

- `text`
- `node_id`
- `bounds`
- `view_id`

The tool should use the target resolver and only focus editable/input nodes.

## User workflow

A user or local agent can first focus a named input field, then call `type_text` with confidence that text will go to the intended place.

## Local-first impact

This strengthens deterministic local form control without needing a cloud model.

## Safety and security considerations

Focusing is lower risk than typing, but it can still prepare a sensitive workflow. The action should remain logged and should not bypass policy for the later text entry.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Add `focus_input` to the Android tool catalog.
- [ ] Resolve target through the Milestone 4 selector/resolver path.
- [ ] Reject non-editable targets with a clear error.
- [ ] Preserve policy, approval, and log behavior.
- [ ] Add unit tests for successful focus, missing target, non-input target, and ambiguous target.

## Alternatives considered

Only add target handling inside `type_text`. That is still useful, but a separate focus primitive gives the agent a safer, inspectable step for multi-action form workflows.

problems.title: fix: harden scroll with target-aware container resolution
problems.issue_body:
Parent: #75

## Problem

The current `scroll` tool scrolls forward or backward on the active screen, but it does not resolve a specific scrollable container.

This can fail or scroll the wrong area on screens with nested lists, sheets, or panes.

## Proposed behavior

Make scroll target-aware where possible.

The hardened scroll flow should:

- resolve scrollable containers from `ScreenContext`,
- support current direction-only behavior as a fallback,
- return a clear failure when no scrollable target exists,
- record the selected scroll target in redacted debug data,
- avoid blind repeated scrolling when the screen does not change.

## User workflow

When a user asks TouchPilot to scroll a page or list, the app should scroll the relevant container or explain that no scrollable area is available.

## Local-first impact

Reliable local control depends on scroll behaving consistently across Android screens.

## Safety and security considerations

Do not use unbounded scrolling. Retry and verification must remain bounded and observable.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Resolver can identify scrollable nodes.
- [ ] `scroll` can use a resolved scrollable target where possible.
- [ ] Direction-only scroll remains compatible.
- [ ] No-scrollable-target failure is explicit.
- [ ] Unit tests cover single container, multiple containers, no container, and unchanged-screen behavior where practical.

## Alternatives considered

Keep global scroll only. Rejected because nested scroll surfaces are common on Android.

problems.title: bug: `_retrieve` ignores `retrieval.backends` config and hardcodes embeddings-primary
problems.issue_body:
**What happened**

`vouch init` writes `config.yaml` with:

```yaml
retrieval:
  backends: ["fts5", "substring"]
```

but `context._retrieve` (`src/vouch/context.py:32`) always calls
`index_db.search_semantic` (embedding) first, regardless of that config — and
the config list doesn't even include `"embedding"`. Meanwhile ROADMAP 0.1 and
the README still say *"Default stays FTS5; embeddings opt-in via config.yaml."*

So the config knob is decorative, the documented default is wrong, and there's
no supported way to force FTS5/substring.

**Expected**

`_retrieve` honors a config setting (e.g. `retrieval.backend: auto|embedding|fts5|substring`,
default `auto` = embedding→fts5→substring), and ROADMAP/README describe the
actual behavior.

**Acceptance**

- Setting the backend in `config.yaml` changes which retrieval path runs.
- `embedding` is a recognized value.
- Docs match behavior.

problems.title: feat: `vouch approve --batch` for scriptable bulk approval
problems.issue_body:
**What you're trying to do**

Approving a review queue is one `vouch approve <id>` per proposal. For CI /
automation (and clearing a backlog) I want to approve a known set in one call.

This complements the interactive `vouch review` (#83) — that's a human walking
the queue; this is non-interactive and scriptable.

**Acceptance**

- `vouch approve <id1> <id2> …` (or `--batch`) approves multiple proposals.
- One audit event per approved artifact.
- Clear all-or-nothing vs best-effort semantics (documented).

problems.title: Add batch approval for reviewed proposals
problems.issue_body:
## What you're trying to do

When I have already reviewed several pending proposals, I want to approve them in one command, because approving each one separately is repetitive and encourages shell workarounds.

## What you've tried

I can run `vouch approve <id>` repeatedly, or use a pipeline like `vouch pending --json | jq -r '.[].id' | xargs -n1 vouch approve`.

That works, but it is clunky, less discoverable, and easier to misuse than a first-class batch approval command.

## Suggested shape

Add batch approval to the existing approve flow:

`vouch approve --batch <proposal-id>... --reason "reviewed together"`

Each proposal should use the same validation, self-approval checks, artifact creation, and audit behavior as single-proposal approval.

## Compatibility considerations

This should be additive and should not break existing KBs. It should not change the object model, on-disk layout, bundle format, or `kb.*` method surface.

Because it preserves the existing review gate and only extends the CLI, it likely should not require a VEP.

## Alternatives

Keep approving proposals one at a time with `vouch approve <id>`.

Use `vouch review` for an interactive queue.

Document a shell pipeline using `vouch pending --json`, though that is less discoverable and easier to get wrong.

problems.title: feat: `vouch fsck` — deeper consistency checks than `doctor`
problems.issue_body:
**What you're trying to do**

`doctor`/`lint` catch surface problems, but several bugs (#78 archived-claim
leakage, #80 missing bundle members) are symptoms of no systematic integrity
pass. I want a thorough checker.

ROADMAP 0.3 lists `vouch fsck`.

**Acceptance**

- Detects: orphaned embeddings, dangling supersede/contradict chains,
  decided-proposal ↔ artifact mismatches, index-vs-file drift.
- Reports each finding with object ids.
- `--fix` is explicitly out of scope for v1.

problems.title: feat: structured JSON logging via `VOUCH_LOG_FORMAT=json`
problems.issue_body:
**What you're trying to do**

Running vouch as a service, I can't easily parse its logs. `VOUCH_LOG_FORMAT` is
referenced in ROADMAP **and** `adapters/generic-mcp/README.md` but isn't
implemented anywhere in `src/`.

**Acceptance**

- `VOUCH_LOG_FORMAT=json` emits one JSON object per log line (level, event,
  actor, object_ids).
- Default (unset) keeps the current human-readable format.
- No behavior change beyond formatting.

problems.title: validation gap: bundle/sync import doesn't enforce Source content-addressing (sha256(content) == id)
problems.issue_body:
## Problem

vouch markets Sources as "content-addressed by sha256" (README "Object model"; "Sources are content-hashed"). `storage.put_source` derives the id from the bytes, and `verify.verify_source` re-checks `sha256(content) == source.id`. But `bundle.import_apply` and `sync.sync_apply` write `sources/<sha>/{meta.yaml,content}` straight from the tarball and never verify that the content hashes to its claimed id. So a hand-built, manifest-consistent bundle can land a Source whose content does not match its content-address.

This is the same structural shape as #81 (uncited claims) and the dangling-reference gap (#123): the invariant is documented and has a detector (`verify_source` / `vouch doctor`), but no write path enforces it.

End-to-end repro on `test` HEAD (696f028):

```
claimed source id : aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa...   (well-formed hex, but a lie)
sha256(content)   : d6cc72573b97385e...   (does NOT match)

import_check.ok = True   issues=[]
import_apply wrote 2 files
landed source: id=aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa...  hash=aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa...
verify_source.stored_ok = False   (KB is now internally inconsistent)
```

The per-file sha256 gate added in #74 does NOT catch this: it only proves each tar member's bytes match the manifest entry, not that the content matches the directory's content-address. The manifest honestly records the real hash of the (attacker-chosen) content bytes, so #74 passes.

Affected paths:

- `bundle.import_check` / `bundle.import_apply` (`src/vouch/bundle.py`) - `_validate_content` explicitly skips `sources/<sha>/content`, and nothing compares `sha256(content)` to `<sha>` or to `meta.yaml`'s `id`/`hash`.
- `sync.sync_check` / `sync.sync_apply` (`src/vouch/sync.py`) - same per-file validation path via `bundle._validate_content`.

## Why this matters

- It breaks the headline "content-addressed by sha256" guarantee on every import/sync path.
- It is exploitable: an attacker can substitute the actual evidence behind a legitimate-looking source id. A claim that "cites source X" then points at bytes that were never hashed to X, while `meta.yaml` still advertises the original hash. The audit log records a clean `bundle.import` / `sync.apply` event, so an operator has no signal the source content was swapped.
- `verify_source` / `vouch doctor` flags `stored_ok=False` only after the inconsistent source has already landed - detection without prevention.

## Suggested fix

Add a per-file content-addressing check on the import/sync validation path (mirrors the `verify_source` invariant, enforced before write):

1. For each `sources/<sha>/content` member, require `sha256(content) == <sha>`.
2. For each `sources/<sha>/meta.yaml`, require `id == <sha>` and (when set) `hash == <sha>`.
3. Wire it into `bundle.import_check` (so `import_apply` inherits the refusal, same as #74/#81) and into `sync`'s per-file validation (so `sync_check`/`sync_apply` refuse).

No on-disk-layout, schema, or bundle-format change - honest bundles produced by `vouch export…

problems.title: feat: vouch expire — garbage-collect stale pending proposals
problems.issue_body:
## What you're trying to do

When multiple agents propose writes over weeks, `.vouch/proposed/` accumulates pending proposals that never get reviewed. Those files are **gitignored**, so they do not show up in PRs and are easy to forget. Reviewers see a growing `vouch pending` list with no clear lifecycle for “we’re never going to look at this.”

I want a first-class way to **expire** old pending proposals: move them to `decided/` with an explicit reason, emit audit events, and honor the config knob that already exists in the init template — without silently deleting history or bypassing the review gate.

**Concrete example:** An agent proposed three claims in March; it is now June and nobody approved or rejected them. Running `vouch expire` should list those three; `vouch expire --apply` should close them as `rejected` with `decision_reason: expired`, so `vouch audit` and `decided/` still explain what happened.

## What you've tried

- **`vouch reject`** — Works per proposal but requires a human reason for each id; does not scale for bulk stale cleanup.
- **Manual deletion of `proposed/*.yaml`** — Loses audit trail and violates the “decided/ is never GCed” model.
- **`review.expire_pending_after_days` in `templates/config.template.yaml`** — Documented (default `90`, `0` = never) but **not read anywhere in code** today.

## Suggested shape

### CLI

```bash
vouch expire              # dry-run: list proposals that would expire
vouch expire --apply      # perform GC
vouch expire --json       # machine-readable output

problems.title: Path Traversal in Bundle Import — Arbitrary File Write (Critical)
problems.issue_body:
The `import_apply` function joins attacker-controlled tar member names directly onto `kb_dir` without sanitizing `../` sequences:

```python
dest = kb_dir / member.name                       # member.name from tar archive
# ...
dest.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
dest.write_bytes(tar.extractfile(member).read())   # writes anywhere on disk
```

The manifest check at line 227 (`if member.name not in recorded`) is circular — the manifest is also inside the attacker-controlled tarball.

### Reproduction

1. Craft a `.tar.gz` where one member has name `../../evil.txt` and `manifest.json` lists it as a valid file.
2. Run `vouch import-apply evil-bundle.tar.gz` or call `kb.import_apply` via JSONL/MCP.
3. `evil.txt` is written two directories above `.vouch/`.

This is the classic CVE-2007-4559 pattern.

### Suggested fix

```python
dest = (kb_dir / member.name).resolve()
if not str(dest).startswith(str(kb_dir.resolve())):
    raise RuntimeError(f"path traversal in bundle: {member.name}")
```

problems.title: Approve Writes Artifact Before Moving Proposal
problems.issue_body:
**Location:** `src/vouch/proposals.py:235-271`

`approve()` writes the durable artifact (e.g. `put_claim`) before updating the proposal status to `APPROVED` via `move_proposal_to_decided`. If the process crashes between the two operations, the proposal remains `PENDING` while the artifact is already on disk. A second approval call would then write a duplicate or silently overwrite with different metadata.

### Suggested fix

Check for artifact existence before writing (idempotent guard), or update proposal status first.

problems.title: Finding 2: Arbitrary File Read via `register_source_from_path`
problems.issue_body:
The `kb.register_source_from_path` method reads the full contents of any file the process can access and stores them in the KB:

```python
path = Path(p["path"])
if not path.is_file():
    raise ValueError(f"not a file: {path}")
src = s.put_source(path.read_bytes(), ...)
```

An agent can register `/etc/passwd`, `~/.ssh/id_rsa`, `~/.aws/credentials`, etc. as a "source", then retrieve the contents via `kb.cite` or `kb.list_sources`.

### Suggested fix

Resolve the path and verify it is inside the project root before reading:

```python
resolved = Path(p["path"]).resolve()
root = _store().root.resolve()
if not str(resolved).startswith(str(root)):
    raise ValueError(f"path must be inside project root: {resolved}")
```

problems.title: fix(cli): approve and reject commands don't catch ProposalError — raw traceback
problems.issue_body:
The CLI `approve` and `reject` commands catch `(ArtifactNotFoundError, ValueError)` but `proposals.approve()` and `proposals.reject()` raise `ProposalError(RuntimeError)` for validation failures. This causes users to see raw Python tracebacks instead of clean error messages.

### Bug
- **File:** `src/vouch/cli.py:192-196` (approve) and `src/vouch/cli.py:204-209` (reject)
- **Buggy code (approve):**
```python
@cli.command()
@click.argument("proposal_id")
@click.option("--reason", default=None)
def approve(proposal_id: str, reason: str | None) -> None:
    store = _load_store()
    try:
        artifact = do_approve(store, proposal_id, approved_by=_whoami(), reason=reason)
    except (ArtifactNotFoundError, ValueError) as e:  # <-- missing ProposalError
        raise click.ClickException(str(e)) from e
```
- **Correct pattern** (from `server.py:359-363` — the MCP server gets this right):
```python
def kb_approve(proposal_id: str, reason: str | None = None) -> dict[str, Any]:
    try:
        artifact = approve(_store(), proposal_id, approved_by=_agent(), reason=reason)
    except (ArtifactNotFoundError, ValueError, ProposalError) as e:  # includes ProposalError
        raise ValueError(str(e)) from e
```

### Steps to Reproduce
1. `vouch init`
2. Propose a claim, get proposal id `P1`
3. `vouch approve P1` (succeeds)
4. Manually copy the decided proposal back to `proposed/` (simulating a retry)
5. `vouch approve P1`
6. **Result:** raw `ProposalError` traceback with "cannot approve: claim X already exists" instead of a clean `Error: cannot approve: ...` message

Alternatively, approve a proposal that's already been approved:
1. `vouch approve <already-decided-id>`
2. **Result:** raw traceback with "proposal X is approved, not pending"

### Expected Behavior
Clean error: `Error: proposal X is approved, not pending` (same format as other CLI errors).

problems.title: fix(verify): wrong exception type caught — verification crashes on missing content
problems.issue_body:
`verify_source()` catches `FileNotFoundError` but `store.read_source_content()` raises `ArtifactNotFoundError` (a `KeyError` subclass) when content is missing. The mismatch means a single source with missing content crashes the entire verification sweep.

### Bug
- **File:** `src/vouch/verify.py:30-31`
- **Buggy code:**
```python
def verify_source(store: KBStore, source: Source) -> VerificationResult:
    try:
        body = store.read_source_content(source.id)
    except FileNotFoundError:  # <-- wrong exception type
        return VerificationResult(source=source, stored_ok=False,
                                  external_status="n/a", note="stored content missing")
```
- **Correct pattern** (from `storage.py:217-221`):
```python
def read_source_content(self, source_id: str) -> bytes:
    p = self._source_dir(source_id) / "content"
    if not p.exists():
        raise ArtifactNotFoundError(f"source content {source_id}")  # KeyError subclass
    return p.read_bytes()
```

### Steps to Reproduce
1. `vouch init && vouch source add some-file.txt` (registers a source)
2. Manually delete `.vouch/sources/<sha256>/content`
3. Run `vouch source verify` or `vouch doctor`
4. **Result:** unhandled `ArtifactNotFoundError` traceback instead of graceful "stored content missing" report

### Expected Behavior
`verify_source` should catch `ArtifactNotFoundError` and return a `VerificationResult` with `stored_ok=False`, as the code clearly intended.

problems.title: bug: agent can self-approve its own proposals — forbidden_self_approval gate not implemented
problems.issue_body:
**Description**

The docs state that by default a proposal cannot be approved by the same actor that filed it. In practice, `kb_approve` in `src/vouch/server.py` passes `_agent()` as `approved_by`, and `proposed_by` on every proposal is also `_agent()` — the same env var. There is no check anywhere in `proposals.approve()` that compares the two. Any agent can therefore propose and immediately approve its own claims with no human in the loop, defeating the core "writes require approval" guarantee described in the README.

`rg "forbidden_self_approval|approver_role"` returns nothing in `src/` and `tests/`.

**Steps to Reproduce**

1. Set `VOUCH_AGENT=alice`
2. Propose a claim via `kb_propose_claim`
3. Call `kb_approve` with the same agent
4. Observe: claim is approved and written to `claims/` with `approved_by="alice"` and `proposed_by="alice"` — no error raised

**Actual Behavior**

The claim is approved silently. The review gate is completely bypassed.

**Expected Behavior**

`proposals.approve()` should raise `ProposalError("forbidden_self_approval")` when `approved_by == proposal.proposed_by`, unless the config sets `review.approver_role` to `"trusted-agent"` to explicitly opt out.

**Environment**

- Python 3.11+
- vouch pre-1.0

problems.title: bug: crystallize() always raises forbidden_self_approval — single-agent sessions cannot be crystallized
problems.issue_body:
**Description**

`sessions.crystallize()` calls `proposals.approve()` with
`approved_by=approver`, where `approver` is `_agent()` — the same value
recorded as `proposed_by` on every proposal filed during the session.

Since `approve()` now enforces the `forbidden_self_approval` guard, every
proposal in the session hits the guard and is dumped into `failures[]`.
Single-agent crystallize silently approves nothing and returns an empty
`approved` list with all proposals in `failures`.

**Steps to Reproduce**

1. Start a session as agent `alice`
2. File one or more proposals within that session
3. Call `kb_crystallize(session_id)` as the same agent `alice`
4. Observe: `approved` is empty, all proposals appear in `failures` with
   `"forbidden_self_approval"`

**Actual Behavior**

All proposals fail silently. No artifacts are written. No error is raised
— the caller just gets an empty `approved` list.

**Expected Behavior**

`crystallize()` is an explicitly trusted operation — the host calls it
knowing the agent is approving its own session. It should either bypass
the self-approval guard or accept a dedicated `trusted=True` flag.

**Environment**

- Python 3.11+
- vouch pre-1.0

problems.title: bug: propose_claim swallows I/O and parse errors in evidence validation — misleading ProposalError raised
problems.issue_body:
**Description**

In `proposals.py`, the evidence validation loop catches bare `Exception`
on `store.get_source()`. Any real error — YAML parse failure, permission
error, disk I/O — is silently swallowed and falls through to
`store.get_evidence()`, which also fails, producing a misleading
`ProposalError("unknown source/evidence id: <eid>")` even when the source
exists but is corrupt or unreadable.

**Steps to Reproduce**

1. Register a source so its id is valid
2. Corrupt its `meta.yaml` (write invalid YAML to the file)
3. Call `kb_propose_claim` citing that source id
4. Observe: `ProposalError("unknown source/evidence id")` — source appears
   missing when it isn't

**Actual Behavior**

Corrupt or unreadable sources are indistinguishable from missing ones.
The real error is hidden behind a misleading message.

**Expected Behavior**

Only `ArtifactNotFoundError` should be caught. All other exceptions
should propagate with their original type and message intact.

**Environment**

- Python 3.11+
- vouch pre-1.0

problems.title: bug: vouch search CLI applies a single backend label to all results and has stale docstring
problems.issue_body:
**Description**

The `search` command in `cli.py` assigns a single `backend` variable
after deciding which code path ran, then prints that same label for
every result in the output:

    try:
        hits = index_db.search(store.kb_dir, query, limit=limit)
        if not hits:
            hits = store.search_substring(query, limit=limit)
            backend = "substring"
        else:
            backend = "fts5"
    except Exception:
        hits = store.search_substring(query, limit=limit)
        backend = "substring"
    for kind, hid, snippet, score in hits:
        click.echo(f"[{kind}] {hid}  score={score:.3f}  ({backend})")

This is the same structural problem that was fixed in `server.py` via
the embedding refactor (PRs #38–#44), but the CLI was never updated.
The `backend` label reflects control flow, not which backend produced
each individual hit.

Additionally, the command docstring still reads:

    """FTS5 search over claims, pages, and entities."""

This is stale — the search layer now supports embedding, FTS5,
substring, and hybrid backends. The docstring misleads users about
what the command actually does.

**Steps to Reproduce**

1. Ensure `state.db` exists but is empty so FTS5 returns no hits
2. Run `vouch search <query>` with a term that matches via substring
3. Observe: all results are labelled `(fts5)` instead of `(substring)`

**Actual Behavior**

All results share one backend label regardless of which backend
produced them. The command docstring advertises FTS5-only behaviour.

**Expected Behavior**

Each result line should carry the backend tag of the backend that
actually produced it. The docstring should reflect the current
multi-backend search surface.

**Environment**

- Python 3.11+
- vouch pre-1.0

problems.title: bug: session_end backfills proposal_ids with all statuses including decided proposals
problems.issue_body:
**Description**

In `session_end()` inside `src/vouch/sessions.py`, the backfill of `proposal_ids` calls `store.list_proposals()` with no status filter:

`sess.proposal_ids = sorted({p.id for p in store.list_proposals() if p.session_id == sess.id})`

`store.list_proposals()` with no argument returns all proposals regardless of status — pending, approved, rejected, and decided. This means `proposal_ids` on the session object includes proposals that were already resolved before `session_end` was called, inflating the count stored in the audit log and shown in the session summary.

**Steps to Reproduce**

1. Start a session and file two proposals
2. Approve one proposal before calling `session_end`
3. Call `session_end`
4. Observe: `proposal_ids` includes both the pending and the already-approved proposal — audit log shows inflated count

**Actual Behavior**

`proposal_ids` includes proposals of all statuses. Audit events and session summaries report inflated proposal counts.

**Expected Behavior**

The audit log count should correctly reflect only proposals that were still pending at session end, not all proposals ever filed in the session.

**Environment**

- Python 3.11+
- vouch pre-1.0

problems.title: bug: crystallize writes session-summary page without FTS5 indexing — pages invisible to kb.search
problems.issue_body:
## Description

In `src/vouch/sessions.py:99-110`, `crystallize()` constructs the session-summary Page and writes it directly via `store.put_page(page)`:

```python
if write_summary_page and approved_artifact_ids:
    page = Page(
        id=f"session-{sess.id}",
        title=f"Session {sess.id}",
        type=PageType.SESSION,
        body=_build_summary_body(sess, approved_artifact_ids),
        claims=[...],
    )
    store.put_page(page)
    summary_page_id = page.id
```

`KBStore.put_page()` only writes the markdown file and calls `_embed_and_store(kind="page", ...)`. The FTS5 side of the index is
populated by `index_db.index_page()`, which is called **only** from `proposals.approve()` on the PAGE branch  `src/vouch/proposals.py:240-247`). Because crystallize bypasses `approve()` for the summary page, FTS5 is never updated.

`rg "index_page" src/vouch/` confirms the only callers are `proposals.approve()` and `health.py`'s rebuilder (used by `vouch index`) — nothing in `sessions.py`.

The retrieval path in `src/vouch/context.py:29-40` and the FTS5 branch in `src/vouch/server.py` short-circuit on the first non-empty FTS5 result, which means in any KB with a populated `state.db`, every `session-<id>` summary page is silently absent from search results. Substring fallback would find them, but the fallback only runs when FTS5 returns nothing — so on a normal-sized KB, summary pages are effectively unsearchable.

This breaks the documented intent of `vouch crystallize` from the README:

> `vouch crystallize` promotes a session's durable parts into proposals;
> one approve and they're permanent.

The summary page is one of the durable parts, but it isn't retrievable through the primary search path that every agent and `kb.context` caller uses.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Initialise a fresh KB and rebuild the FTS5 index so `state.db` has rows from at least one other artifact:
   ```bash
   vouch init
   vouch source add ./some-file.md
   # propose + approve at least one claim/page so state.db is non-empty
   vouch index
   ```
2. Start a session, file a proposal, approve via crystallize:
   ```bash
   SID=$(vouch session start --task "demo" --json | jq -r .id)
   vouch propose-claim --text "demo claim" --source <sid> --session "$SID"
   vouch session end "$SID"
   vouch crystallize "$SID"
   ```
3. Confirm the summary page exists on disk:
   ```bash
   ls .vouch/pages/session-*.md      # present
   ```
4. Search for any token from the summary page title or body:
   ```bash
   vouch search "Session $SID"       # no page hit
   ```
5. Force the substring fallback (e.g. delete `state.db`) and re-run the
   same search — the page now appears.

## Actual Behavior

The session-summary page is written to disk and embedded, but the FTS5 table has no row for it. `vouch search`, `kb.search`, and the FTS5 branch of `kb.context` all miss the page. Only the substring fallback (which only fires when FTS5 returns zero results) can find it.

problems.title: bug: crystallize silently swallows approval failures and exits 0 on complete failure
problems.issue_body:
**Description**

`crystallize()` in `src/vouch/sessions.py` wraps every `approve()` call in a bare `except Exception` that catches all failures and appends them to a `failures` list. When every proposal in a session fails to approve, the function returns `{"approved": [], "failures": [...]}` and the CLI prints the JSON and exits with code 0 — giving no indication that the operation completely failed.

A human running `vouch crystallize <session_id>` expecting their proposals to become durable artifacts gets a silent no-op with a success exit code. The failures are only visible if they inspect the JSON output carefully.

**Steps to Reproduce**

1. Start a session and file one or more proposals
2. Trigger a condition where `approve()` raises — e.g. a storage error or a status conflict
3. Run `vouch crystallize <session_id>`
4. Observe: exits 0, prints JSON with `"approved": []` and failures buried in `"failures"` array

**Actual Behavior**

All approval failures are swallowed silently. The CLI exits 0 regardless of whether any proposals were actually approved. The user has no indication that crystallize failed.

**Expected Behavior**

If all proposals fail to approve, `crystallize` should exit with a non-zero code and surface a clear error. Partial failures should at minimum print a warning so the user knows some proposals were not committed.

**Environment**

- Python 3.11+
- vouch pre-1.0

problems.title: bug: vouch export produces self-invalid bundles on Windows (manifest uses backslash separator, tar uses forward slash)
problems.issue_body:
## What happened

On Windows, `vouch export` writes bundle paths into `manifest.json`
using the native `\` separator while `tarfile` records member names
with `/`. Every bundle vouch produces on Windows is therefore
self-invalid:

- `manifest.counts` is `{claims: 0, pages: 0, sources: 0, ...}` for
  every KB regardless of content — the per-subdir counter at
  `src/vouch/bundle.py:90` uses `f["path"].startswith(f"{sub}/")`,
  which never matches `sources\...`.
- `vouch export-check` on the bundle vouch just produced returns
  `ok: false` with `file in bundle but not in manifest` for every
  tar member and `manifest lists missing file` for every manifest
  entry.
- `vouch import-apply` is a silent no-op: the manifest-paths set
  lookup at `src/vouch/bundle.py:264` is keyed on the `\`-flavoured
  strings, so every tar member is treated as "not in manifest" and
  skipped.

Portable bundles are a load-bearing feature of vouch (README:
"Portable bundles"), so this corrupts the only mechanism for moving
a KB between repos on any Windows host.

## What you expected

`vouch export` produces a bundle whose `manifest.json` matches the
member names inside the tarball, `vouch export-check` reports
`ok: true`, and `vouch import-apply` restores every artifact —
behaviour identical to Linux/macOS.

## Reproduction

Minimum sequence on Windows (PowerShell, Python 3.12, vouch 0.0.1
at commit `c3accb6`, the merge of #46):

```powershell
PS> $env:PYTHONUTF8 = '1'            # separate Windows bug, needed for `vouch --help`
PS> $env:VOUCH_AGENT = 'repro-bot'
PS> mkdir $env:TEMP\vouch-repro; cd $env:TEMP\vouch-repro

PS> vouch init
Initialised KB at C:\Users\Administrator\AppData\Local\Temp\vouch-repro\.vouch

PS> 'doc body' | Out-File -Encoding utf8 doc.txt
PS> vouch source add doc.txt --title 'Repro doc'
f87b4431a0ad0a4bb9f002bd56cfa9ab07791783684ca3df80be249d434134f4

PS> vouch export --out kb.tar.gz
{
  "bundle_id": "bee1189d498a3539b4c695ac7b81b1a386e4d2bff6c68edf481e26e9a409f7c4",
  "files": 3,
  "out": "kb.tar.gz"
}

PS> vouch export-check kb.tar.gz
{
  "bundle_id": "bee1189d...",
  "files_checked": 3,
  "issues": [
    "file in bundle but not in manifest: sources/f87b4431.../content",
    "file in bundle but not in manifest: sources/f87b4431.../meta.yaml",
    "manifest lists missing file: sources\\f87b4431...\\content",
    "manifest lists missing file: sources\\f87b4431...\\meta.yaml"
  ],
  "ok": false
}
```

The asymmetry between `sources/...` (tar member) and `sources\...`
(manifest path) is the root cause.

A round-trip with `vouch import-apply` into a fresh `.vouch/`
writes nothing — every member is silently skipped because its
`/`-shaped name isn't in the manifest's `\`-shaped path set.

The pytest suite hits the same bug via
`tests/test_bundle.py::test_export_import_round_trip` and
`tests/test_bundle.py::test_import_apply_skips_conflicts_by_default`,
both of which fail on Windows but pass on the Linux-only GitHub
Actions matrix (`.github/workflows/ci.…

problems.title: bug: import_check / import_apply never verify member sha256 against manifest — bundle integrity gate is bypassable
problems.issue_body:
## What happened

`bundle.import_check` and `bundle.import_apply` never verify each
tar member's content against the per-file `sha256` recorded in
`manifest.json`. The integrity guarantee that the README markets
("tar.gz + manifest.json with per-file sha256") is therefore only
enforced by `export_check`; the *import* side trusts whatever is
in the tarball as long as the path appears in the manifest and the
schema parses.

An attacker (or an accidental disk-corruption event) who substitutes
a tar member's bytes without re-signing the manifest can land
arbitrary content into a `.vouch/` KB:

- The audit log records a successful `bundle.import` event with
  the legitimate `bundle_id` — there is no trace that the bytes
  ever differed from the manifest.
- For YAML/markdown artifacts the attacker only has to ship a
  payload that passes `pydantic` validation. Claim text,
  confidence, status, citations, page bodies, entity attributes,
  relation targets — all are attacker-controlled in a single
  shipped bundle.
- For `sources/<sha>/content` files `_validate_content` skips
  validation entirely, so source content can be replaced with
  arbitrary bytes. `Source.sha256` in `meta.yaml` remains the
  legitimate hash, so the resulting KB is internally inconsistent
  (`vouch source verify` would now fail), but the import itself
  succeeds.

This defeats the documented integrity story of vouch's portable
bundle format on every platform.

## What you expected

`vouch import-check` and `vouch import-apply` reject any bundle
whose member bytes don't hash to the per-file `sha256` in
`manifest.json` — the same check that `vouch export-check` already
performs. `import_apply` raises before writing a single byte when
the bundle has been tampered with.

## Reproduction

```bash
$ python tamper_repro.py
work dir: /tmp/vouch-tamper-XXXXXXXX
original claims/c1.yaml sha256: bccff52f5cf3cf9e...
tampered claims/c1.yaml sha256: bb996b9aa4bd7058...

export_check(tampered): ok=False
  issues: ['hash mismatch: claims/c1.yaml']

import_check(tampered): ok=True
  issues: []

import_apply(tampered): wrote 4 files

landed c1.text: 'TAMPERED the sky is red'
landed claims/c1.yaml sha256: bb996b9aa4bd7058...
manifest sha256 for that path:  bccff52f5cf3cf9e...

BUG CONFIRMED: import_apply wrote attacker content; on-disk hash
does not match the manifest sha256.
```

`tamper_repro.py` exports a one-claim bundle, rewrites the tarball
so that `claims/c1.yaml` contains a different (but schema-valid)
claim body, leaves `manifest.json` byte-for-byte unchanged, and
imports into a fresh KB. The substituted text lands in the
durable artifact and the audit log records a clean
`bundle.import` event with the original `bundle_id`.

The asymmetry is at:

- `src/vouch/bundle.py:139` — `export_check` reads each member,
  hashes it, and reports `hash mismatch: <name>` on disagreement.
- `src/vouch/bundle.py:231` — `import_check` reads each member
  only to feed `_validate_content` (schema check), never…

problems.title: bug: crystallize writes a durable Page bypassing the review gate; body embeds agent-controlled markdown verbatim
problems.issue_body:
## What happened

`sessions.crystallize` writes a durable `Page` artifact directly via
`store.put_page(page)` (`src/vouch/sessions.py:99-110`), bypassing
the `proposals.propose_page` → `proposals.approve` review gate that
guards every other write to `pages/`.

The body of that page is built by `_build_summary_body`
(`src/vouch/sessions.py:125-139`) and embeds three
agent-controlled fields verbatim into rendered markdown:

- `sess.task` — set by the agent at `session_start(task=...)`
- `sess.note` — set/extended by the agent at `session_start` /
  `session_end`
- `sess.agent` — taken from the `VOUCH_AGENT` env var of the proposing
  agent

There is no escape, no sanitisation, no length cap, and no
review step between the agent supplying these strings and the
durable markdown page landing in `pages/session-<id>.md`. The page
is committed to git on the next `git add .vouch/`, indexed (#60
notwithstanding, the page row still lands in the artifact set), and
returned by `kb.read_page`, `kb.list_pages`, and `kb.context`.

The audit event recorded at `src/vouch/sessions.py:112-116`
lists `[sess.id, *approved_artifact_ids]` as `object_ids` — the
new page id is **not** in the list. So the audit log is also
not truthful about what was written.

This breaks the load-bearing review-gate guarantee
("Writes require approval", README §"Why this exists" point 2 and
CONTRIBUTING §"Things we won't merge" point 1) for the Page
artifact kind. An ordinary agent can land arbitrary markdown
into a reviewed KB by:

1. `kb.session_start(task="<any markdown payload, headings, lists,
   even fake **Decision:** rows>")`.
2. Filing any one legitimate proposal in that session.
3. Waiting for *any* approver to crystallize the session
   (which they would do precisely because the trivial claim looks
   fine in `vouch show`).

The approver reviews the claim, not the session task — but their
approval action causes a page to land whose body content was
unilaterally chosen by the agent.

## What you expected

The session-summary page is either:

(a) **Strictly derived** from already-approved content — only the
    session id, the list of crystallized claim ids, the approver
    name, and timestamps. Nothing the proposing agent supplied is
    promoted into a durable page body. *Or*

(b) **Routed through `propose_page` + `approve`** so the human
    reviewer can see and reject the body before it lands.

Either way, `vouch approve` (or a `crystallize` invocation that
internally goes through `approve`) is the only path to a durable
`pages/*.md`. And the audit event for `session.crystallize`
includes the page id in `object_ids` whenever a page is written.

## Reproduction

```bash
$ vouch init
$ export VOUCH_AGENT=mallory
$ SID=$(vouch session start --task '## DECISION

We will migrate to MongoDB. Approved by leadership 2026-05-25.

- This page was never reviewed by a human.
- An ordinary agent caused this content to land in the KB.

```python
print("agent-controlled python prose")
```
…

problems.title: bug: kb.context returns archived/superseded claims; lifecycle mutations never re-index FTS5 status
problems.issue_body:
## What happened

`kb.context` returns claims that have been `archived`, `superseded`,
`contested`, or `redacted` as if they were live — agents receive
retracted knowledge in their context pack. Two compounding bugs
combine to produce this:

**Bug A — `build_context_pack` has no `status` filter.**
`src/vouch/context.py:75-101` walks the hits from `_retrieve` and
appends every match to the context pack. There is no check on
`claim.status`. Once a claim is indexed, it stays in retrieval
forever, regardless of subsequent lifecycle calls.

**Bug B — `store.update_claim` never refreshes the FTS5 row.**
`src/vouch/storage.py:308-313`:

```python
def update_claim(self, claim: Claim) -> Claim:
    if not self._claim_path(claim.id).exists():
        raise ArtifactNotFoundError(f"claim {claim.id}")
    self._claim_path(claim.id).write_text(_yaml_dump(claim.model_dump(mode="json")))
    self._embed_and_store(kind="claim", id=claim.id, text=claim.text)
    return claim
```

The embedding cache is refreshed; the FTS5 row is not. So
`lifecycle.archive`, `lifecycle.supersede`, and
`lifecycle.contradict` — all of which finish with
`store.update_claim(...)` and never themselves call
`index_db.index_claim` — leave `claims_fts.status` stuck at whatever
value it had at first-index time.

`index_db.index_claim` is called by `proposals.approve` (at
`src/vouch/proposals.py:258`) on first approval and by
`health.rebuild_index` during `vouch index` / `vouch doctor`. No
update path keeps it in sync with `lifecycle` mutations.

The compound effect: even a future fix that adds a status filter
to `context.py` would still leak archived claims, because the FTS5
`status` column it would filter on is stale. Both bugs must be
fixed for retrieval to be correct.

This breaks the read-side promise of the KB. The whole point of
`ClaimStatus.{ARCHIVED, SUPERSEDED, REDACTED}` is to remove a
claim from active circulation while keeping its history. Today
those states are decorative.

## What you expected

`kb.context` (and the underlying `build_context_pack`) should
exclude claims whose `status` is `ARCHIVED`, `SUPERSEDED`, or
`REDACTED` from the returned items. `CONTESTED` claims may be
surfaced (per `kb.lint` and `vouch lint`, "contested" is a
caution, not a removal), but the host should be able to see
their status — adding `status` to `ContextItem` lets agents and
hosts decide.

`lifecycle.archive`, `lifecycle.supersede`, and
`lifecycle.contradict` should keep `claims_fts.status` in sync
with the on-disk claim — either by calling `index_claim` directly
or by making `store.update_claim` re-index the FTS5 row in
addition to refreshing the embedding cache.

## Reproduction

```
$ python archived_context_repro.py
work dir: /tmp/vouch-archived-XXXXXXXX

--- after approval (stable claim) ---
items: [('mongodb-is-faster-than-postgres', '«MongoDB» is faster than Postgres')]

--- claim mongodb-is-faster-than-postgres archived ---
on-disk claim.status: archived
FTS5 row after archive: id=mongod…

problems.title: bug: Claim model has no min-evidence validator — uncited claims land via bundle import, put_claim, update_claim
problems.issue_body:
## What happened

The `Claim` model has no min-evidence validator
(`src/vouch/models.py:184-187`), so a claim with `evidence: []` is
schema-valid. As a result, the README and CONTRIBUTING contract —

> Claims must cite sources. A claim without at least one Source /
> Evidence id is a **validation error**, not a warning.

is enforced in exactly one place: `proposals.propose_claim`
(`src/vouch/proposals.py:89-90`). Every other write path to
`claims/<id>.yaml` accepts uncited claims silently:

- **Bundle import** — `bundle._validate_content` defers to
  `Claim.model_validate` (`src/vouch/bundle.py:43,222-228`), which
  accepts `evidence: []`. A hand-crafted bundle with one such claim
  passes `import_check` (`ok=True`, no issues) and `import_apply`
  writes it to disk under the legitimate `bundle_id`. The audit
  log records a clean `bundle.import` event for a KB that now
  contains an uncited claim.

- **`store.put_claim` direct** — `src/vouch/storage.py:283-291`
  iterates `claim.evidence` to verify each cited id exists, but an
  empty list iterates zero times. Lifecycle ops
  (`supersede`, `contradict`, `archive`, `confirm`) and any caller
  that constructs a `Claim` directly can land an uncited one.

- **`store.update_claim`** — `src/vouch/storage.py:308-…` writes
  the YAML without any citation check. A previously-cited claim
  can be mutated to `evidence=[]` and the change persists.

This is exactly the failure mode CONTRIBUTING calls out under
"Things we won't merge":

> Validation relaxations that let claims land without citations.
> A claim without evidence is a `working` note at best — register
> a source.

— except the enforcement was never on the model in the first
place, so every write path *except* the one explicit gate already
qualifies as the relaxed shape the team won't accept.

## What you expected

Constructing a `Claim` with `evidence=[]` raises `pydantic.ValidationError`.
That closes all three bypass paths in one stroke:

- `bundle.import_apply` raises (via `_validate_content`) before
  writing.
- `store.put_claim(Claim(evidence=[]))` raises at the `Claim(...)`
  call.
- `store.update_claim(claim_with_emptied_evidence)` raises the
  moment a caller mutates `claim.evidence = []` and tries to pass
  it back through `Claim(...)` or pydantic re-validation.

The duplicate check inside `propose_claim`
(`if not evidence: raise ProposalError(...)`) can stay as the
nicer user-facing error message, but the load-bearing invariant
moves to the model where it belongs.

## Reproduction

```
$ python uncited_repro.py
work dir: /tmp/vouch-uncited-XXXXXXXX
Bypass 1 (put_claim): SUCCEEDED — uncited claim on disk
  YAML on disk has 'evidence: []' → True
Bypass 2 (update_claim): SUCCEEDED — citations stripped to []

bundle.import_check(uncited): ok=True issues=[]
bundle.import_apply: wrote 1 files
landed claim text: 'shipped via bundle, no citations'
landed claim evidence: []

BYPASS 3 CONFIRMED: bundle import landed an uncited claim — the
README c…

problems.title: feat: Add vouch review for walking through pending proposals
problems.issue_body:
## What you're trying to do

When reviewing agent-proposed knowledge, I want a guided queue that walks me through pending proposals one at a time, because the current flow requires manually copying proposal IDs between `vouch pending`, `vouch show`, `vouch approve`, and `vouch reject`.

This would make the review-gate workflow faster while preserving the human approval step.

## What you've tried

Today I can run:

- `vouch pending`
- `vouch show <proposal-id>`
- `vouch approve <proposal-id>`
- `vouch reject <proposal-id> --reason "..."`

That works, but it is repetitive when several proposals are pending.

## Suggested shape

Add a CLI command:

```bash
vouch review

problems.title: bug: import_check accepts bundles whose manifest lists files missing from the tarball (no issue raised)
problems.issue_body:
## What happened

`bundle.import_check` walks two loops in series: one over manifest
entries (to compute `new_files` / `conflicts` / `identical`), and one
over tar members (to schema-validate and now — after #74 / #75 — to
verify the per-member sha256). Neither loop flags the case where
`manifest.json` lists a path that has **no corresponding tar member**.

The symptom is the inverse of `hash mismatch` and is just as silent:

- `import_check` returns `ok=True` for a bundle where, say,
  `manifest.json` claims `claims/c1.yaml` exists with a particular
  sha256, but the tarball contains no `claims/c1.yaml` member.
- `import_apply` then iterates tar members, never reaches the
  manifest-listed-but-missing path, and writes nothing for it. The
  `bundle.import` audit event records a clean success with the
  bundle id, claiming "imported the bundle" — but the resulting KB
  is missing artifacts the manifest promised.

`export_check` already catches this (it walks recorded paths and
checks `tar.getmember(path)`). The same companion check is missing
on the import side — exactly the asymmetry #74 was about, just for a
different attack/corruption shape (omission instead of substitution).

## What you expected

`import_check` should append a `manifest lists missing file: <path>`
issue for any manifest entry without a matching tar member,
mirroring `export_check`. `import_apply` then refuses to import
because `check.issues` is non-empty.

## Reproduction sketch

```python
# Build a bundle whose manifest references claims/c1.yaml but the
# tarball contains only manifest.json.
manifest = {
    "spec": bundle.SPEC_VERSION,
    "bundle_id": "deadbeef",
    "files": [{
        "path": "claims/c1.yaml",
        "size": 16,
        "sha256": hashlib.sha256(b"text: any
").hexdigest(),
    }],
    "counts": {},
    "safety": {"has_proposed": False, "has_state_db": False, "has_audit_log": False},
}
with tarfile.open("missing.tar.gz", "w:gz") as tar:
    mf = json.dumps(manifest).encode()
    info = tarfile.TarInfo(bundle.MANIFEST_NAME)
    info.size = len(mf)
    tar.addfile(info, io.BytesIO(mf))

diff = bundle.import_check(store.kb_dir, "missing.tar.gz")
# Today: diff.ok is True. Expected: diff.ok is False with
# a "manifest lists missing file" issue.
```

## Environment

- vouch version: post-#75 (or any version, since this code path
  is untouched by #72 / #74 / #75)
- Cross-platform; reachable from CLI / MCP / JSONL

## Suggested fix

In `src/vouch/bundle.py` `import_check`, after collecting
`manifest_paths` and walking the manifest entries for the dest
diff, add the missing-member pass that `export_check` already has:

```python
member_names = {
    m.name for m in tar.getmembers()
    if m.isfile() and m.name != MANIFEST_NAME
}
for path in manifest_paths - member_names:
    issues.append(f"manifest lists missing file: {path}")
```

(or equivalently `try: tar.getmember(path) except KeyError: ...`
to mirror `export_check`'s shape exactly).

Add a regression test i…

problems.title: feat: Add `--json` output to `vouch pending`
problems.issue_body:
## What you're trying to do

When I’m reviewing proposals in a multi-agent repo, I want to inspect pending proposals in a machine-readable format so I can group them by agent, count noisy proposal sources, or wire the review queue into shell scripts and CI checks.

The docs already suggest this workflow:

```bash
vouch pending --json | jq -r '.[] | "\(.proposed_by)\t\(.id)"' | sort | uniq -c
```
but vouch pending --json does not appear to exist yet.

## What you've tried
vouch status --json gives a machine-readable summary, but it only includes a pending proposal count, not proposal details.

vouch pending lists pending proposals, but the output is human-readable text, so scripts would need to parse display formatting.

The MCP/JSONL kb.list_pending surface can return structured pending proposal data, but that is heavier than needed for simple CLI automation.

## Suggested shape
Add a --json flag to vouch pending.

Expected behavior:

- vouch pending keeps its current human-readable output.
- vouch pending --json emits a JSON array of pending proposals.
- If there are no pending proposals, output [] and exit 0.
- Proposal entries can use the existing proposal model shape, likely Proposal.model_dump(mode="json").

Suggested test coverage:

- pending proposals render as valid JSON
- empty pending queue renders as []
- existing human-readable output remains unchanged
## Compatibility considerations

This should be additive and should not break existing KBs.

It should not require a VEP if the change stays limited to CLI output. It does not change the object model, kb.* method surface, on-disk layout, bundle format, audit-log shape, or transport behavior.

problems.title: feat: Add deterministic sync/merge for diverged .vouch/ directories
problems.issue_body:
## What you're trying to do

When multiple agents, teammates, branches, or repos each have their own `.vouch/` directory, I want a deterministic way to reconcile them, because today the multi-agent story works best on one shared filesystem.

For example, Team A and Team B may both approve claims in separate repos, then later want to combine their reviewed knowledge. Right now that means bundle export/import or git merge, but conflicts still need manual interpretation and there is no first-class “what will happen if I merge these KBs?” workflow.

## What you've tried

Existing bundle features help move a KB snapshot:

- `vouch export`
- `vouch import-check`
- `vouch import-apply`

These are useful for portability, but they do not fully solve distributed KB convergence. They can identify new, identical, and conflicting files, but they do not provide a semantic merge workflow for diverged `.vouch/` directories, decided proposals, superseded claims, contradictions, or audit implications.

Git merge is also possible, but it leaves vouch-specific conflict handling up to the user.

## Suggested shape

Add a first-class sync/merge workflow, for example:

```bash
vouch sync-check <path-or-bundle>
vouch sync-apply <path-or-bundle> --on-conflict fail|skip|propose

problems.title: Abstract `Assistant` class, in preparation for OpenClaw integration
problems.issue_body:
Move the current `Assistant` to a module - and current existing functionality to `Assistant:Builtin` as per this Claude Code architecture ... summary of the plan:

## Architecture Overview

### Before

`Assistant` was a single monolithic PORO class that handled everything: LLM provider lookup, configuration, function calling, response streaming, and broadcasting.

### After

`Assistant` is now a **module with a registry/factory pattern**:

```
Assistant (module)
  ├── .for_chat(chat)      → routes to the right implementation via Family preference
  ├── .config_for(chat)    → backward compat delegation to Builtin
  ├── .available_types     → ["builtin", "external"]
  │
  ├── Base                 → abstract class with shared Broadcastable concern
  ├── Builtin              → current behavior (zero changes), uses Provided + Configurable
  └── External             → stub for future WebSocket/OpenClaw assistants
```

### Key Design Decisions

1. **Module + Registry** — follows the existing `Provider::Registry` pattern. New assistant types just need a class and a REGISTRY entry.
    
2. **Family-level preference** — `families.assistant_type` column (default `"builtin"`) lets each family choose their assistant. `Chat#assistant` resolves through the factory transparently.
    
3. **Clean interface boundary** — `Base` only requires `for_chat(chat)` and `respond_to(message)`, plus shared `Broadcastable`. External assistants don't need local LLM providers, functions, or the Responder.
    
4. **Zero breaking changes** — all existing callers (`Chat#assistant`, `Assistant.config_for`, `Settings::AiPromptsController`) work unchanged.

problems.title: feat: Address uniqueness constraints of account numbers for people that link same account
problems.issue_body:
> _⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_
> 
> <details>
> <summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>
> 
> 🏁 Script executed:
> 
> ```shell
> #!/bin/bash
> # Check Mercury account model for validations and constraints
> echo "=== Mercury account model ==="
> fd -t f "mercury_account.rb" app/models --exec head -100 {}
> 
> echo -e "\n=== Mercury account processor/integration code ==="
